schemaNote: Masters' principles for voicing selection (#220). Each principle is paraphrased in the maintainer's own words. References cite sources for further study. voicingStyleTags / playStyleTags map to the Stage-1-reserved voicing schema fields; engine consumption follows in a Track-3 ticket. Initial principles drafted from general knowledge — the project owner will refine and expand.

masters.name: George Van Eps
masters.instrument: 6-string (the 1939 Method) and later 7-string (low A; Harmonic Mechanisms, Mel Bay, 1980-82)
masters.biography: Pianistic master of the guitar whose body of method work began with the 1939-era Epiphone-published Van Eps Method (6-string) and matured into the 3-volume Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar (Mel Bay, 1980-1982) on the 7-string with low A. The principles below are sourced from the 6-string 1939 method (primary source verified). 7-string-specific principles from Harmonic Mechanisms (lap-piano style, independent bass counterpoint on the low A) are pending a separate research pass.

principles.name: Harmonized scale as foundational vocabulary
principles.summary: Every chord-quality category in Van Eps's method begins by harmonizing the corresponding scale in triads. Major chords (Ex. 1-25) start with the harmonized major scale in triads; minor chords (Ex. 26-32) with the harmonized minor; 7th chords (Ex. 33-47) with the 7th-arpeggio scale; diminished chords (Ex. 48-82) with the harmonized diminished. The harmonized scale IS the vocabulary; voicings derive from running it through different fingerings. Practice in all 12 keys via the solfeggio system: 'Think of the tonic of every key as do.'

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Foreword + Ex. 1 (major) / Ex. 26 (minor) / Ex. 33 (7th) / Ex. 48 (diminished). Quoted: 'All exercises presented in this volume have been carefully tested through years of teaching. Each one has a definite purpose for development of the hand, no matter how insignificant it may seem to the student.' Solfeggio quote also from Foreword.

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: Chapter 2, 'The Mighty Triads'; Chapter 12, 'The Chromatic Scale as Universal Source'.
references.topic: Triad as central unit (substrate of the entire book)
references.quote_excerpt: Most of the studies in these books are based on, and relate to the “triad” in one form or another

applied_lessons.name: 1939 harmonized major scale in 3-voice triads (6-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Ex. 1, p. 8 (harmonized major scale in triads)
applied_lessons.notes: 1939 Ex. 1 walks closed-position triads up the harmonized major scale; the typical 6-string assignment uses three adjacent strings per fingering form (sets 1|3, 2|3, 3|3). Register-compressed: bass and inner share the lower strings.

applied_lessons.name: Vol 1 'Mighty Triads' as four-voice voicing (7-string)
applied_lessons.notes: Vol 1 ch. 2 treats the triad as substrate for four-part SATB-shaped voicings on 7-string: bass on the low-A 7th string, inner voices stratified on 5/6 and 3/4, top on 1/2. Register-distinct (no collision between bass and inner-low).

principles.name: Six fingering forms across string sets
principles.summary: Every chord pattern is practiced in up to six different fingerings on different string-set positions: sets of 3 (1|3, 2|3, 3|3, 4|3), sets of 4 (1|4, 2|4, 3|4), and 'broken' sets (1|B3, 2|B3, 3|B3 — sets that skip an intermediate string). The fingerings cover the entire fingerboard with the same harmonic content. The six forms of Ex. 1 cover roughly: 1st form C-up-to-F; 2nd C-to-C#(D); 3rd C-to-F; 4th Ab-to-Db(D); 5th Ab-to-E; 6th F#-to-C#. Practiced legato; finger placement is 'next-position-forming while the hand is in motion' (legato principle).

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Explanation of String Chart (p. 6) and Ex. 1 instructions (p. 8). Each set has a notation symbol (1|3, 2|3, ..., 1|B3, etc.). The six-form scope is documented in the Ex. 1 instructions: '1st form C up to F, 2nd form C up to C sharp (D if possible)...'

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: Chapter 6 / Chapter 12, 'String set symbol system' and 'Broken and divided sets'.
references.topic: String-set symbol system and broken/divided sets
references.quote_excerpt: When the Ist-2nd-4th-strings are employed (skipping the 3rd string) it is termed a broken set

applied_lessons.name: 1939 String Chart six-form fingerings (6-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: String Chart, p. 6; Ex. 1 fingering-form instructions, p. 8
applied_lessons.notes: 1939 documents three adjacent string sets (1|3, 2|3, 3|3) traversing the fingerboard. Each set is one of six fingering forms; the register window shifts up the neck as the set moves toward the bass strings. No broken/divided sets in 1939 — those arrive in Vol 1.

applied_lessons.name: Vol 1 String Set Symbol System (7-string adjacent + broken + divided)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Chapter 12, p. 258 ('String Set Symbol System'); Chapter 6 broken-set introduction
applied_lessons.notes: Vol 1 formalizes adjacent sets (1/3, 2/3, 3/3, 4/3), broken sets that skip one intermediate string (1/B3, B1/3, etc.), and divided sets that skip two (D1/4, 1/D3). Register-strings shown here are the typical assignment; exact strings depend on which set is selected and which voice carries which line.

principles.name: Outer-voice anchor with inner voice motion
principles.summary: Hold outer voices (top or bass) while inner voices move — Van Eps's central polyphonic device. Documented across multiple exercises: Ex. 14 ('the upper melodic line is in half notes while the two lower voices are in whole notes'); Ex. 20 (top quarter notes, bottom whole notes); Ex. 57 ('the top line is in quarter notes and the harmonic structure is in whole notes'); Ex. 60-65 ('the moving voice is in the middle of the structure'); Ex. 82 ('the third and fourth fingers play the two upper voices in quarter notes while the first and second fingers sustain the lower voices in whole notes'). Pre-figures the 'lap piano' / Harmonic Mechanisms aesthetic.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Exercises 14, 18, 20, 37, 40, 42, 46, 57, 60-65, 67-68, 82. Ex. 82: 'Individual control of the fingers is developed in this exercise. In the first measure the third and fourth fingers play the two upper voices in quarter notes while the first and second fingers sustain the lower voices in whole notes.'
references.instrument_caveat: On 6-string (1939), the 'lower voices' are typically a single bass voice plus one inner voice, with inner motion constrained to alto register. The Vol 1 4-voice realization (bass on low A + two independent inner voices + top) is not portable to 6-string without significant register compression.

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: Chapter 6, 'Sustain pressure while articulating' (lap-piano texture demand).
references.quote_excerpt: keep the pressure applied firmly while the quarter notes are in motion

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: Chapter 4, 'Hold the whole note while voice moves' (anchor-and-move drill).
references.topic: Anchor-and-move drill: sustain whole note while other voice runs the scale
references.quote_excerpt: Run the moving voice up and down

applied_lessons.name: 1939 Ex. 82 outer-anchor with inner motion (6-string, 3-voice)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Ex. 82 (last diminished exercise; 'third and fourth fingers play the two upper voices in quarter notes while the first and second fingers sustain the lower voices in whole notes')
applied_lessons.notes: Three-voice texture: a single bass voice and a single top voice are sustained while one inner line animates in the alto register. Bass and inner share the lower strings and frequently the same hand position — the 6-string register cramp Van Eps documents but does not solve until the 7-string.

applied_lessons.name: Vol 1 ch. 4 anchor-and-move (7-string, 4-voice with 7th-string pedal)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Chapter 4, p. 70 ('Run the moving voice up and down repeatedly in each station while making sure to sustain the whole note'); Chapter 6, p. 134 ('keep the pressure applied firmly while the quarter notes are in motion')
applied_lessons.notes: Four-voice realization: bass holds pedal on the 7th string while two independent inner voices animate above it and the top voice sustains. The two inner voices are stratified across the 5/6 and 3/4 string zones — only possible because the bass has vacated those strings for the 7th.

principles.name: Chromatic 10ths with middle voice motion
principles.summary: Specific stretching-exercise mechanic from Ex. 37: hold a 10th interval between outer voices while the middle voice moves chromatically. Van Eps's own description: 'the middle note remains the same while the other two voices move around the middle voice in chromatic tenths.' Uses a 'broken set of three strings' (one string between the two outer voices is skipped). Each form is then transposed to all 12 keys.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939), Ex. 37
references.citation: Direct quote from Ex. 37 instructions: 'The middle note remains the same while the other two voices move around the middle voice in chromatic tenths. This is the first exercise using a broken set of three strings.'
references.instrument_caveat: Ex. 37 in 1939 establishes the chromatic-tenths-with-sustained-middle-voice device, but on 6-string the register footprint is constrained — the device is workable but compressed. Vol 1 chapters 5 and 7 expand the same device into proper register distribution with the 7th string carrying the bass independent of the chromatic tenths.

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: Chapter 5, 'Sixths opening up to tenths'; Chapter 7, chromatic third-to-tenth exercises.
references.quote_excerpt: Sixths opening up to tenths

applied_lessons.name: 1939 Ex. 37 chromatic 10ths with sustained middle voice (6-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Ex. 37 ('the middle note remains the same while the other two voices move around the middle voice in chromatic tenths'; first exercise using a broken set of three strings)
applied_lessons.notes: Broken set of three strings (one intermediate string skipped) carries the 10th interval. Register-compressed on 6-string: the outer voices move in parallel chromatic 10ths while the middle voice is held; the bass voice cannot walk independently because it is locked into the lower side of the 10th.

applied_lessons.name: Vol 1 ch. 5/7 sixths-opening-to-tenths with 7th-string bass (7-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Chapter 5 ('Sixths opening up to tenths'); Chapter 7 chromatic third-to-tenth exercises
applied_lessons.notes: Vol 1 lifts Ex. 37's device into register-distinct territory: the chromatic-tenths pair occupies inner-low and top, the sustained middle remains in the inner-high zone, and the bass is freed onto the 7th string to walk or pedal independently of the 10ths.

principles.name: Pulsation picking with top-voice emphasis
principles.summary: Right-hand technique: pick passes over each string with 'a slight kick, which is more of a pulsation' rather than picking each note individually. Wrist axis is positioned directly over the highest note 'as the top note should predominate.' Built-in dynamic stratification across a triad: '...the D string will sound softly, the G string a little louder, and the B string will be the loudest, which is dynamically correct.' Inside-string playing uses the next-higher string as a pick-stop on downstrokes.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: 'Pick and Wrist Action' section (p. 3) + Ex. 2 instructions (p. 9). Ex. 2: 'The pick passes over each string and accents it with a slight kick, which is more of a pulsation... using the pulsation principle you will be able to maintain a steady tempo and you will not strike two strings at once.' Picking section: '...the top note should predominate.'

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., 1939.
references.citation: Generic citation of the 1939 method as primary source for the pulsation-picking right-hand mechanic with top-voice dynamic emphasis; Harmonic Mechanisms Vol 1 (2016) treats right-hand attack only in passing (e.g., ch. 6 'left and right hand attack must be together') and does not name pulsation picking. No Vol 1 corollary.

applied_lessons.name: 1939 pulsation picking with top-voice dynamic emphasis (6-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: 'Pick and Wrist Action' section, p. 3; Ex. 2 instructions, p. 9 ('The pick passes over each string and accents it with a slight kick, which is more of a pulsation')
applied_lessons.notes: Right-hand technique rather than a voicing shape per se. The signature consumed by the engine is: a 3-voice closed voicing where the top voice is dynamically emphasized via wrist position; D string sounds softly, G louder, B loudest. No Vol 1 corollary — pulsation picking is not named in Vol 1.

principles.name: Finger flattening for added notes ('fifth finger')
principles.summary: Left-hand technique: deliberately flatten the first joint of a finger from its arched position to produce an additional note on a neighboring string, classified as a 'fifth finger' addition. Introduced in Ex. 16 ('the breaking, or flattening from an arched position, of the first joint of the fingers'); difficult because the flattening finger must continue to sustain another note during the process. Practiced 'with a snap,' rhythmically reliable. Voicings designed with this technique fit one more note than the four fingers alone could reach.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939), Ex. 16
references.citation: Direct quote from Ex. 16: 'This exercise must be practiced very carefully as we introduce a new principle in the first two forms which is the breaking (or flattening from an arched position) of the first joint of the fingers. In the first form at the second and third measures, you flatten the first joint of the second finger to produce the added note, which brings this principle into the classification of a fifth finger.'

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: Chapter 12, 'THE FIFTH FINGER PRINCIPLE'; Chapter 9, second-finger flatten-then-arch hammer mechanic.
references.topic: Fifth Finger Principle (one finger, two non-collinear notes)
references.quote_excerpt: the knack of sounding two notes simultaneously that are not located on the same fret, with one finger

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: Chapter 9, 'Second-finger hammer mechanic' (joint-level flatten-then-arch).
references.topic: Joint-level flatten-then-arch mechanic for sustained inner voice
references.quote_excerpt: The first joint of the second finger plays the double stop in flattened position

applied_lessons.name: 1939 Ex. 16 finger-flattening seed (6-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Ex. 16 ('the breaking, or flattening from an arched position, of the first joint of the fingers... flatten the first joint of the second finger to produce the added note, which brings this principle into the classification of a fifth finger')
applied_lessons.notes: A four-note voicing fit on 6-string only because the second finger flattens its first joint to cover one extra string at the same fret — a 'virtual fifth finger.' Without the flatten, only four fingers and four notes; with it, the voicing accommodates a fifth chord tone.

applied_lessons.name: Vol 1 ch. 12 Fifth Finger Principle formalized (7-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Chapter 12, p. 263 ('the knack of sounding two notes simultaneously that are not located on the same fret, with one finger'); Chapter 9, p. 185 (joint-level execution: 'The first joint of the second finger plays the double stop in flattened position')
applied_lessons.notes: Vol 1 generalizes the flatten beyond same-fret adjacent-string: the second-finger first joint sounds two notes on different frets ('not located on the same fret'). On 7-string with the bass relegated to the 7th string, the flattened finger is free to operate higher in the voicing without contending for the bass register.

principles.name: String deadening for open voicings
principles.summary: Left-hand technique for open voicings on non-adjacent strings: the pick strikes a string while a left-hand finger touches (deadens) it to silence the note. Ex. 58 documents the mechanic precisely: 'the D string (IV) is stopped from vibrating with the second finger while that finger is used for the note on the A (V) string. (i.e. The pick strikes that string, but the left hand finger does not let it sound clearly.)' Enables voicings where the pick can sweep cleanly across all strings while only the intended notes ring.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939), Ex. 58
references.citation: Direct quote from Ex. 58 (the first diminished open-voicing exercise) and its footnote: 'In the first form the D string (IV) is stopped from vibrating with the second finger while that finger is used for the note on the A (V) string... *i.e. The pick strikes that string, but the left hand finger does not let it sound clearly.'

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., 1939.
references.citation: Generic citation of the 1939 method as primary source for string-deadening as an enabler of open voicings. Harmonic Mechanisms Vol 1 (2016) addresses spacing-for-audibility (ch. 1) and selective string-set choice as the structural mechanism for open voicings rather than naming string-deadening as a discrete technique. No Vol 1 corollary.

applied_lessons.name: 1939 Ex. 58 string-deadening for open diminished voicings (6-string)
applied_lessons.source_citation: Ex. 58 footnote ('the D string (IV) is stopped from vibrating with the second finger while that finger is used for the note on the A (V) string... The pick strikes that string, but the left hand finger does not let it sound clearly')
applied_lessons.notes: Enables open voicings on non-adjacent strings while the pick sweeps cleanly across all strings: a left-hand finger touches the intermediate (skipped) string to silence it. The voicing's sounding voices live on non-adjacent strings; the muted string is structural-skipped, not unused. No Vol 1 corollary — Vol 1 reaches the same openness via string-set selection (broken/divided sets) rather than deadening.

works.title: The George Van Eps Method for Guitar
works.summary: Van Eps's primary-source pedagogy. 42-page method published by Epiphone Inc. The method's organizational principle is the harmonized scale — every chord-quality category begins by harmonizing the corresponding scale in triads, practiced across six string-set fingerings, in all 12 keys per the Solfeggio principle. Layered on top are Van Eps's signature techniques: outer-voice anchor with chromatic inner motion (the 'lap piano' texture), pulsation picking with top-voice emphasis, and left-hand finger-flattening and string-deadening as extensions of the standard four-finger reach.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939). 42 pages.
references.citation: Primary source verified in PR #238; predecessor research doc at docs/research/masters/george-van-eps.md.

systems.name: Harmonized-Scale Foundation
systems.summary: Every chord-quality category in Van Eps's method begins by harmonizing the corresponding scale in triads. Major chords (Ex. 1-25) start with the harmonized major scale; minor chords (Ex. 26-32) with harmonized minor; 7th chords (Ex. 33-47) with the 7th-arpeggio scale; diminished chords (Ex. 48-82) with harmonized diminished. The harmonized scale IS the vocabulary; voicings derive from running it through six string-set fingerings, practiced in all 12 keys via the Solfeggio principle.

members.name: First Form
members.summary: Played on the second and first string sets; long form; key range C up to F.

members.name: Second Form
members.summary: Played entirely on the second string set; long form; key range C up to C# (D if possible).

members.name: Third Form
members.summary: Played on second and first string sets with crossover on a different scale degree than Form 1; long form; key range C up to F.

members.name: Fourth Form
members.summary: Played on the third, second, and first string sets; long form; key range Ab up to Db (D if possible).

members.name: Fifth Form
members.summary: Condensed form; key range Ab up to E.

members.name: Sixth Form
members.summary: Most condensed form; key range F# up to C#.

members.name: Harmonized Minor Scale Forms
members.summary: Six parallel forms applied to the harmonized minor scale, including broken string sets, open voicings, and diminished variants.

traversal_rules.name: String-set traversal of harmonized scale
traversal_rules.summary: A harmonized scale moves across string sets preserving the triad relationships. Ex. 1 works the harmonized C-major scale through six string-set forms.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Method p.6 'Explanation of the String Chart' + Ex. 1 instructions (p.8): six-form scope from 1st-form to 6th-form covering the fingerboard.

traversal_rules.name: All-keys practice doctrine (Solfeggio)
traversal_rules.summary: Each harmonized scale form must be practiced in all 12 keys before moving to the next form.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Foreword: 'Think of the tonic of every key as do.' Solfeggio principle as the practice-sequencing rule.

traversal_rules.name: Legato Shift Between Forms
traversal_rules.summary: Position changes between forms must be executed without sliding, with a momentary pressure release and the next formation pre-built during the hand's motion.

references.quote_excerpt: changes from formation to formation must be executed in the least amount of time
Do not slide the left hand in the modulation. It should be a fast, accurate change.

traversal_rules.name: String-Set Crossover Between Forms
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between forms that cross string sets, the octave jump must be executed cleanly with fingers landing surely, distinguishing long-jump hazard (extended forms) from cross-string hazard (condensed forms).

references.quote_excerpt: crossing over the sets of strings to jump the octave presents a different problem as this must be done cleanly

traversal_rules.name: Sequence-Form Traversal Pattern
traversal_rules.summary: Within each form, the harmonized scale is traversed in a three-steps-up-and-back pattern, ascending then reversing descending.

references.quote_excerpt: three steps up and return on the first triad, then, on the second triad, etc., ascending; the reverse, descending

traversal_rules.name: Diminished Symmetry Four-Fret Jump
traversal_rules.summary: Diminished voicings repeat every four frets, so traversal between them uses the same fingering transposed up or down by four frets with fast, slide-free jumps.

references.topic: Diminished chord repeats every four frets
references.quote_excerpt: the diminished chord repeats itself every four frets. Therefore it is necessary to have a good foundation in order to judge the distance between triads.

traversal_rules.name: Rocker-Arm Legato Transition for Diminished Triads
traversal_rules.summary: Alternating first and fourth fingers like a rocker arm achieves legato across diminished triad sequences without sliding or sounding all four notes simultaneously.

references.quote_excerpt: The first and fourth fingers should alternate like the ends of a rocker arm. In other words the first finger should remain down until the fourth comes down

traversal_rules.name: Two-Octave Form Linkage
traversal_rules.summary: After all six forms are perfected individually, lower and upper forms are joined without a break to produce a seamless two-octave harmonized scale.

references.quote_excerpt: After all the forms have been perfected join them up with the harmonized scale an octave higher and practice without a break.

modification_rules.name: Substitute within harmonized-scale equivalents
modification_rules.summary: A I-chord voicing in C major admits substitutes from the harmonized-scale (Em7 as I-function, Am7 as I-function). Triadic-first framing distinguishes this from Greene's 4-note chord-scale-equivalence.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Method's I/iii/vi function-sharing pattern as the practical implementation of the harmonized-scale-first pedagogy.

modification_rules.name: Barre-Finger Substitution Across Fingers
modification_rules.summary: Any barre chord position must be practiced with each finger (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th) performing the barre in turn, expanding the technique beyond a single default finger.

references.quote_excerpt: the second triad should be played not only with a Barre of the fourth finger as marked, but also with Barres of the third, second and first fingers alternately

modification_rules.name: Seventh-Arpeggio Range Truncation
modification_rules.summary: Full-fingerboard arpeggio forms that exceed the instrument's range are modified by omitting the first measure descending (if too low) or the fifth measure ascending (if too high).

references.quote_excerpt: can be moved up and down the fingerboard by leaving out the first measure when descending (if too low) and leaving out the fifth measure when ascending (if too high)

systems.name: Inner-Voice Independence
systems.summary: Van Eps's defining 'lap piano' texture — outer voices anchor while inner voices move with strict contrapuntal independence. Chromatic inner motion is the FIRST preference (distinct from Pass's common-tone-first and Greene's stepwise-inner-first). Each voice treated as an independent contrapuntal line, not as parts of a chord that happen to land at the same time.

members.name: Outer voices (bass + top)
members.summary: Anchor role: hold or move slowly

members.name: Inner voices (alto + tenor)
members.summary: Motion role: carry the harmonic motion

members.name: Chromatic inner motion
members.summary: Default motion class

traversal_rules.name: Outer voices anchor while inner voices carry motion
traversal_rules.summary: Bass and top voice hold or move slowly while inner voices carry the harmonic motion.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Ex. 14, 18, 20, 37, 57, 60-65, 67-68, 82 — documented across the Method per PR #238 research doc.

traversal_rules.name: Inner voices move chromatically by default
traversal_rules.summary: Not just stepwise — chromatic. Ex. 37 (chromatic 10ths with middle voice) is the canonical demonstration.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Ex. 37: chromatic 10ths between outer voices with middle voice carrying chromatic motion.

traversal_rules.name: Each voice has its own contrapuntal line
traversal_rules.summary: Voices are independent contrapuntal lines, not parts of a chord that happen to coincide. The 'pianistic' framing.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Foundational framing across the Method; the lap-piano analogy Van Eps repeats throughout.

modification_rules.name: Voicings convert preserving voice independence
modification_rules.summary: Adjacent voicings must be reachable without breaking the multi-voice texture.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Implicit constraint across the worked exercises; shared with Greene's R4.5 per cross-master comparison in the rebuild notes.

modification_rules.name: Prefer 10ths between outer voices in chromatic-inner contexts
modification_rules.summary: Ex. 37 specifically uses chromatic 10ths between bass and top voice with middle voice carrying motion.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Ex. 37: '10ths' is the outer-voice interval setup that enables chromatic middle-voice motion.

modification_rules.name: Inner-Voice Sustain Under Moving Melody
modification_rules.summary: Inner voices must sustain their full written value while the melodic line moves, enforcing voice independence within each form.

references.quote_excerpt: Make sure they sustain their full value. Practice all three forms equally as the purpose throughout this method is balanced technique.

modification_rules.name: Contrary Motion Voice Expansion
modification_rules.summary: Two voices are set in contrary motion — moving in opposite directions simultaneously — to stretch the hand's span as a modification of standard parallel-motion form traversal.

references.quote_excerpt: Contrary motion is introduced in this stretching exercise. This comes more under the heading of hand gymnastics.

systems.name: Right-Hand Pulsation Technique
systems.summary: Van Eps's pulsation-picking technique with top-voice dynamic emphasis. Wrist axis pivots over the top voice, producing rhythmic pulsation; picking accents the highest sounding pitch. Documentation-only in v1; engine integration is future work (likely surfaces as a playStyleTag on selected voicings when Van Eps is active).

members.name: Arpeggio Picking (Pulsation)
members.summary: Downstrokes pulsed with a slight kick, stopped against the next-highest string; upstrokes return freely without a stop.

members.name: Alternate Picking
members.summary: Downstrokes on beats 1 and 3, upstrokes on beats 2 and 4 within a triad measure.

members.name: Arc-Path Pick Return (Open Voicing)
members.summary: Pick travels a small arc around the upper string after a downstroke to avoid deadening a sustaining voice before striking the middle note.

traversal_rules.name: Pulsation accent on top voice
traversal_rules.summary: Right-hand wrist pivots over the top voice; picking accents the highest sounding pitch.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Method p.3 'Pick and Wrist Action' + Ex. 2 instructions.

traversal_rules.name: Picking-Mode Assignment by Exercise Type
traversal_rules.summary: Arpeggio picking is the default for harmonized scale studies; alternate picking is assigned specifically to second and fourth triads in minor contexts.

references.quote_excerpt: Give this exercise the same treatment as Ex. 1, such as arpeggio picking, etc.
this exercise uses alternate picking, which means that the second and fourth triads in the measure are played with up-strokes

traversal_rules.name: Up-Stroke Retrospective Revision Drill
traversal_rules.summary: All prior down-stroke-only exercises are revisited with up-strokes and then with alternating strokes, requiring up-strokes to match down-stroke speed and tone.

references.quote_excerpt: Go back through the book now, taking all exercises in which down-strokes only were used and practice them using up-strokes. Then practice them using up and down strokes alternately.

traversal_rules.name: Dynamic Axis Rule (Wrist Over Highest Note)
traversal_rules.summary: The wrist axis is positioned directly over the highest note being played so that the top voice predominates dynamically across all chord voicings and positions.

references.quote_excerpt: The axis of your wrist should be directly over the highest note as the top note should predominate.

modification_rules.name: Arc-Path Modification for Open-Voicing Contexts
modification_rules.summary: In open-voicing exercises where a sustained voice sits above the target string, the pick-return path is modified from a straight return to a small arc around the upper string.

references.quote_excerpt: After the down-stroke the pick should travel in a small returning arc around the upper string so you will not touch it and stop its vibration.

systems.name: Left-Hand Mechanics (Flattening + Deadening)
systems.summary: Finger-flattening as virtual fifth finger (a single fretting finger flattens across adjacent strings to add a note without changing finger count); string-deadening for open voicings (specific strings dampened to allow open-string sympathetic notes without unwanted strings ringing).

modification_rules.name: Add note via finger flattening
modification_rules.summary: Single fretting finger flattens across two adjacent strings to add a sounding note. Voicings exceeding four-finger count become accessible. Van Eps treats this as a virtual fifth finger.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Method exercises that introduce 5-note voicings without five distinct fingers. The 'fifth finger' technique is Van Eps's own framing.

modification_rules.name: String deadening enables open-voicing patterns
modification_rules.summary: Specific strings dampened by the fretting hand to allow open-string sympathetic notes without unwanted strings ringing. Used in open voicings where the voicing requires specific bass-treble separation.

references.source: Van Eps, George. The George Van Eps Method for Guitar. Epiphone Inc., NY (ca. 1939)
references.citation: Method's open-voicing exercises where the dampening pattern is part of the voicing's executable form.

systems.name: Solfeggio All-Keys Transposition System
systems.summary: The Solfeggio system is the method's key-equality framework: every exercise is conceived relative to 'do' (the tonic of whatever key is being played), and this relativization obligates the student to practice each exercise in all twelve keys without favoring any. The system is not a separate exercise block but a governing rule applied to every form, every technique, and every exercise introduced in all three chapters. It prevents the common pedagogical failure of privileging easy keys (C, G, D) at the expense of the rest. The method's key-range tables per fingering form (Chapter 2) define where each form can be transposed within this system.

members.name: Tonic as 'Do'
members.summary: Each key's tonic is treated as 'do', making all twelve keys structurally identical in the method's framework.

members.name: Twelve-Key Practice Cycle
members.summary: Every exercise is practiced in all twelve keys; the key-range tables per form define the practical limits of transposition on the guitar.

traversal_rules.name: Key-Equality Traversal Rule
traversal_rules.summary: No key receives privileged practice attention; the student moves through all twelve keys without stopping to favor familiar keys.

references.quote_excerpt: Through this system all keys are equal and therefore you will not favor any particular key or keys.

traversal_rules.name: Form-Range-Bounded Transposition
traversal_rules.summary: Each fingering form is transposed only within its prescribed key range; transpositions outside that range require switching to a different form.

references.quote_excerpt: 1st form—from C up to F … 4th form—from A flat up to D flat (D if possible) … 6th form—from F sharp up to C sharp

works.title: Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar (3 volumes, Mel Bay, 1980-1982)
works.summary: 7-string content pending PDF acquisition. Will contain: diagonal barre technique (Van Eps's fret-10+ innovation); 7th-string bass walking with the low-A as a bass-line resource; inner-voice independence extended to SATB+ topology (6- or 7-voice textures); the 'harmonic mechanisms' approach as Van Eps's mature systematization of inner-voice motion across 7-string voicings.

works.title: Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1
works.summary: Van Eps's canonical 334-page primary-source method, published by Mel Bay (2016 reissue of the mid-1980s original three-volume set). The book treats the guitar as a polyphonic instrument capable of supporting three or four independent contrapuntal lines — Van Eps's "lap-piano" ideal made systematic. The pedagogical arc moves from triadic harmony through four-part chord construction to inner-voice motion exercises, then to the six fingering forms across string sets (including "broken" sets that skip an intermediate string), and culminates in dedicated polyphonic exercises. Three load-bearing systems are derived: inner-voice-independence (outer-voice anchor with chromatic inner motion), six-string-set-fingerings (the harmonized-scale practice across 1|3, 2|3, 3|3, 4|3, 1|4, 2|4, 3|4, and broken sets), and four-part-chord-construction (the tertian stacking taxonomy that underlies all the harmonic material). The method is the canonical complement to the 1939 method already in this master entry — sibling work[].

references.source: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Volume 1. Mel Bay Publications (2016 reissue; original mid-1980s).
references.citation: 334-page method; OCR-sourced via the cyberpower OCR branch (#315/#316/#318). 14 detected chapter-sized sections.

systems.name: Inner-Voice Independence (Lap-Piano Polyphony)
systems.summary: Van Eps's load-bearing system: the guitar is treated as a 'lap piano' in which outer voices (bass and soprano) function as harmonic anchors while inner voices (alto and tenor) move chromatically and contrapuntally beneath them. Every chord exercise is a four-part polyphonic texture in which each voice carries an independent melodic line; the student practices sustaining outer-voice pedal tones while inner voices walk in half-steps or pass through chromatic neighbors. This is the contrapuntal foundation that distinguishes Volume 1 from block-chord method books.

members.name: Outer-Voice Anchor (Bass + Soprano)
members.summary: Bass and soprano lines held or moved deliberately while inner voices animate the texture.

members.name: Chromatic Inner-Voice Motion (Alto + Tenor)
members.summary: Alto and tenor walk in half-steps or pass through chromatic approach tones beneath sustained outer voices.

members.name: Four-Part Voice-Leading Exercises
members.summary: Polyphonic exercises where each of four voices carries an independent melodic line.

members.name: Pedal-Tone Textures
members.summary: Held bass or soprano pedals against moving inner harmony.

members.name: Contrary-Motion Voice Pairs
members.summary: Two voices moving in opposite directions to maintain independence.

traversal_rules.name: Anchor Outer Voices While Inner Voices Move
traversal_rules.summary: Sustain bass and soprano as harmonic anchors; allow alto and tenor to carry the chromatic motion.

references.quote_excerpt: outer voices function as harmonic anchors while inner voices move

traversal_rules.name: Move Inner Voices in Half-Steps
traversal_rules.summary: Inner voices (alto and tenor) advance by chromatic half-step or chromatic neighbor approach beneath stable outer voices.

references.topic: Chromatic passing tones in inner voices
references.quote_excerpt: inner voices walk in half-steps or pass through chromatic neighbors

traversal_rules.name: Each Voice Carries an Independent Line
traversal_rules.summary: Treat every chord progression as four simultaneous melodic lines, not as a stack of blocks.

references.quote_excerpt: each voice carries an independent melodic line

modification_rules.name: Insert Chromatic Approach in Inner Voices
modification_rules.summary: Where harmony permits, insert a chromatic passing tone in alto or tenor to smooth the voice-leading without disturbing outer-voice contour.

references.quote_excerpt: pass through chromatic neighbors beneath sustained outer voices

systems.name: Six String-Set Fingering System
systems.summary: Van Eps organizes the fretboard into six distinct four-string sets (strings 1-2-3-4, 2-3-4-5, 3-4-5-6, plus the three 'broken' sets 1-2-3-5, 1-2-4-5, 1-3-4-5 etc.) and drills harmonized scales through each set. The harmonized-scale practice runs every diatonic chord of a key through one string set before moving to the next, producing systematic coverage of every voicing on every region of the neck. Broken (non-adjacent) string sets are emphasized because they introduce the string-skipping geometry needed for the polyphonic textures of System 1.

members.name: Adjacent Four-String Sets (1-2-3-4, 2-3-4-5, 3-4-5-6)
members.summary: The three contiguous four-string groupings used as the primary fingering scaffold.

members.name: Broken (Non-Adjacent) String Sets
members.summary: String sets with skipped strings (e.g., 1-2-3-5, 1-3-4-6) that demand string-skipping right-hand technique.

members.name: Harmonized-Scale Drill
members.summary: Each diatonic chord of a key voiced on one string set and run through the scale before changing sets.

members.name: Position Shifts Between Sets
members.summary: Mechanical fingering shifts used to transition between string sets within a single phrase.

traversal_rules.name: Cycle Through All Six String Sets
traversal_rules.summary: Run every harmonized-scale and chord exercise through all six string sets (three adjacent, three broken) before considering the lesson complete.

references.quote_excerpt: six distinct four-string sets drilled in turn

traversal_rules.name: Harmonize the Scale on One Set Before Changing Sets
traversal_rules.summary: Complete every diatonic chord of the key on a single string set before transitioning to the next set.

references.quote_excerpt: every diatonic chord through one string set before moving

traversal_rules.name: Drill Broken Sets for String-Skipping Geometry
traversal_rules.summary: Practice non-adjacent string sets with deliberate string-skipping right-hand articulation to support polyphonic textures.

references.quote_excerpt: broken sets introduce string-skipping geometry

modification_rules.name: Preserve Left-Hand Position Across Set Changes
modification_rules.summary: When transitioning between string sets, prefer fingerings that keep the left hand in the same neck region.

references.quote_excerpt: systematic coverage of every voicing on every region of the neck

systems.name: Four-Part Chord Construction (Triads to Extended Chords)
systems.summary: Van Eps builds the harmonic vocabulary sequentially: triads first, then tetrads (seventh chords), then extended chords (9ths, 11ths, 13ths), grouped by chord-quality category (major, minor, dominant, diminished, augmented, half-diminished). Every triad is presented in all inversions on every string set, then 'completed' by adding a fourth voice to convert it to a tetrad. The system treats extended chords as substitution-expansions of their parent tetrads: a Cmaj9 is taught as Cmaj7 with the 9th replacing or doubling an inner voice.

members.name: Triad Inversions on Every String Set
members.summary: Root, first, and second inversions of major and minor triads voiced on each string set.

members.name: Tetrad Family (Seventh Chords)
members.summary: Maj7, mi7, dom7, dim7, half-dim7, aug7 - the four-voice quality categories.

members.name: Extended Chords (9, 11, 13)
members.summary: Upper-extension voicings built by substituting an extension for a chord tone within a parent tetrad.

members.name: Chord-Quality Categories
members.summary: Major, minor, dominant, diminished, augmented, half-diminished as the six top-level family bins.

members.name: Triad-to-Tetrad Conversion
members.summary: Adding a fourth voice to a triad to produce its tetrad form within the four-part texture.

traversal_rules.name: Build Triads → Tetrads → Extensions in Order
traversal_rules.summary: Master every inversion of every triad on every string set before introducing tetrads, and every tetrad before introducing extended chords.

references.quote_excerpt: triads first, then tetrads, then extended chords

traversal_rules.name: Group Voicings by Chord-Quality Category
traversal_rules.summary: Practice all major-quality chords together, all minor-quality together, etc., to internalize the family of shapes.

references.quote_excerpt: grouped by chord-quality category

modification_rules.name: Substitute an Extension for an Inner Chord Tone
modification_rules.summary: Convert a tetrad into an extended chord by replacing an inner voice (typically the root or 5th) with the 9th, 11th, or 13th.

references.quote_excerpt: 9th replacing or doubling an inner voice

modification_rules.name: Convert Triad to Tetrad by Adding a Fourth Voice
modification_rules.summary: Promote any triad voicing to its tetrad by adding the characteristic 7th (or 6th) on an available string within the current string set.

references.quote_excerpt: completed by adding a fourth voice to convert it to a tetrad

masters.name: Ted Greene
masters.biography: Los Angeles teacher and player whose lessons and the openly-distributed tedgreene.com archive reshaped how guitarists think about four-note voicings, voice-leading, and the relationship between melody and harmony. Authored Chord Chemistry (1971) and Modern Chord Progressions (1973). His V-System — a taxonomy of 14 voicing groups across 43 unique four-note chord qualities — is the canonical Greene framework.

principles.name: V-System voicing groups
principles.summary: Four-note voicings are classified into 14 groups (V-1 through V-14) ordered from most compact to most spread: V-1 is the closest cluster, V-14 the widest, with V-13/V-14 less spread than V-11/V-12. Any chord quality (43 unique four-note qualities) can be voiced in any V-group; cycling through them gives every distinct way to spell the same chord. The framework is taxonomic, not prescriptive — Greene's pragmatic note: 'use logic to generate possibilities but don't be so strict that you lose [practical results].'

references.source: tedgreene.com V-System archive (Hober's intro)
references.citation: Hober, James. V-System Introduction. tedgreene.com. Corpus extract at plugin/data/masters-corpus/greene/01_WhoIsJamesHober.txt.

principles.name: Fixed soprano tour
principles.summary: Hold the top voice (soprano) constant on a chosen melody note; cycle through all 14 V-System voicing groups to reveal every way Greene's system harmonizes underneath that note. For chord-melody work this is the engine: the melody fixes the soprano; the V-System provides the harmonic exploration. Hober: 'in a single line of music notation he had concisely described and implied the entire V-System.'

references.source: tedgreene.com V-System archive (Hober's 'Who Is James' chapter)
references.citation: Hober, James. Who Is James? — V-System Introduction. Corpus extract at plugin/data/masters-corpus/greene/01_WhoIsJamesHober.txt.

principles.name: Voice leading through inner motion
principles.summary: Melody stays on top while inner voices (alto, tenor) move stepwise between chord changes. The bass anchors, the top voice carries the melody, and the inner voices do the harmonic work — producing the sense of multiple independent lines rather than block chords. The V-System's 'Conversion' method (Welcome chapter index, item 20) is the formal device for moving between V-groups while preserving voice-leading economy.

references.source: Chord Chemistry
references.citation: Greene, Ted. Chord Chemistry. Dale Zdenek, 1971.

references.source: tedgreene.com V-System archive
references.citation: Welcome chapter; corpus extract at plugin/data/masters-corpus/greene/00_Welcome.txt.

principles.name: ii7 preferred over IVmaj7 for the IV function
principles.summary: Greene's 1974 manuscript on ii7-V7-I documents the historical/aural preference for ii7 over IVmaj7 in the IV-V-I cadence: IVmaj7's root-to-7 major-7 interval is strident, while ii7's minor-7 interval is mild. The substitution defines the canonical jazz ii7-V7-I cadence; voicings supporting the ii7-V7-I motion (smooth root motion by 5ths, common-tone voice leading) are preferred. Minor variant: iiø7 - V(7) - i.

references.citation: Manuscript. Corpus extract at plugin/data/masters-corpus/greene/ii7-V7-I_TedGreene_1974-11-28_and_29.txt. Quoted: 'the strongly dissonant interval of a major 7th... was apparently harsher to the ears of our forefathers than the mild dissonance of a minor 7th in the ii7.'

principles.name: Sequential articulation
principles.summary: Voicings are played with right-hand fingerstyle or hybrid-pick articulation rather than strummed — bass plucked first, inner voices in sequence, melody on top. The pluck order is part of the music; strumming the same shape produces a different effect. Closely associated with Greene's harp-harmonics technique (also Lenny Breau), which he treasured and lamented as overexposed (1995 letter).

references.source: Modern Chord Progressions
references.citation: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions. Dale Zdenek, 1973.

references.source: Ted Greene letter to Bruce (1995-12-16)
references.citation: Manuscript. Corpus extract at plugin/data/masters-corpus/greene/3-NoteChordsForThe_I_ChordInGBlues_WithLetter_TedGreene_1995-12-16.txt — Greene laments harp-harmonics losing their impact through overexposure.

systems.name: V-System (14 Four-Note Voicing Groups)
systems.summary: Greene partitions all 4-note voicings into 14 V-groups ordered by compactness (V-1 is the closest cluster; V-14 the widest, though V-13/V-14 are LESS spread than V-11/V-12 — a Greene-specific quirk). Each of the 43 unique 4-note chord qualities has exactly one instance per V-group. The fixed-soprano tour — hold the melody note constant and cycle through all 14 V-groups — is the canonical chord-melody engine for Greene-active sections.

traversal_rules.name: V-group cycling generates all 14 voicings of a quality
traversal_rules.summary: A given chord quality can be expressed in any of the 14 V-groups. Cycling generates all 14.

references.source: James Hober V-System tutorials at tedgreene.com
references.citation: Corpus: 01_WhoIsJamesHober.txt + V-System exposition. Cross-ref Chord Chemistry (1971).

traversal_rules.name: V-group Conversion: move one note by octave to shift V-group
traversal_rules.summary: Greene's 'Conversion' method — moving one note up or down by an octave shifts the voicing to a different V-group. The method specifies which moves correspond to which transitions.

references.source: tedgreene.com V-System archive (estate-authorized PDFs).
references.citation: Hober's tutorials enumerate the conversion table.

traversal_rules.name: Fixed soprano tour — cycle V-groups underneath a held melody note
traversal_rules.summary: Hold the top voice constant on a chosen melody note; cycle through all 14 V-groups to reveal every way the system harmonizes underneath. The canonical chord-melody engine for Greene-active sections.

references.source: tedgreene.com V-System archive.
references.citation: Hober's 'Who Is James' chapter; the canonical pedagogical use of the V-system.

modification_rules.name: Density floor: 4 sounding notes (V-system by definition)
modification_rules.summary: V-System is by definition 4-note voicings. 3-note variants belong to a separate system.

references.source: V-system definition (Hober + Chord Chemistry).
references.citation: Constitutive of the V-system identity.

modification_rules.name: Substitute V-group for V-group within chord position
modification_rules.summary: When voicing a chord, V-group choice is preferred per phrase context — closer V-groups for inner-voice movement; wider V-groups for register variety.

references.source: tedgreene.com archive.
references.citation: Worked examples across the corpus.

systems.name: Chord-Scale Equivalence (Substitution Lattice)
systems.summary: For each chord quality in a key, a list of equivalent voicings derived from the chord scale. In C major, the I (Cmaj7) admits voicings drawn from the C-major chord-scale; substitutes include Em7 (iii7) as a I-function chord. The equivalence class is open vocabulary, derived per-chord from the diatonic chord-scale system.

members.name: V-chord equivalents (G7, its altered family, sus variants)

traversal_rules.name: String transference
traversal_rules.summary: A voicing on one string set transfers to another string set preserving relative chord position. Makes Greene's substitutions also positional.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: 'Diatonic Chord Scales (Major Keys) and String Transference' section; 7 occurrences in OCR.

traversal_rules.name: Within-chord-scale equivalents share notes (common-tone)
traversal_rules.summary: Moving between chord-scale equivalents is fluid; equivalents share notes by construction.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Inherent property of diatonic chord-scale derivation.

modification_rules.name: Substitute any chord-scale equivalent for the written chord
modification_rules.summary: Any chord in a chord-scale equivalence class may substitute for any other. Broader than functional substitution.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Distinguishing Greene's framework from functional-harmony substitution.

modification_rules.name: Chord-scale equivalence applies tune-wide
modification_rules.summary: Like Pass's R5.3 tune-wide alteration permission, Greene's chord-scale equivalence applies across the whole tune in a key.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Pervasive application throughout MCP's worked examples.

systems.name: Diatonic Substitution Lattice (Cycle Patterns)
systems.summary: Named progression patterns from Modern Chord Progressions — distinct from chord-scale equivalence (which is at the level of single chords). Cycles include I-vi-ii-V, iii-vi-ii-V (the ii7-over-IVmaj7 preference), diatonic cycle of 5ths in major and minor keys, all-dominant III7-VI7-II7-V7, mixed-mode cycles, tritone subs.

members.name: Diatonic cycle of 5ths in major keys

traversal_rules.name: Tonicization at each cycle position
traversal_rules.summary: Each chord in a cycle can be tonicized — becomes a dominant-7 of its successor (producing III7-VI7-II7-V7).

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: 'III7 VI7 II7 V7' cycle chapter.

modification_rules.name: Substitute one cycle variant for another
modification_rules.summary: I-vi-ii-V → iii-vi-ii-V (the ii7-over-IVmaj7 preference); III7-VI7-II7-V7 → I-VI7-II7-V7 (mixed mode); tritone substitution on any dominant.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: MCP's catalog of cycle variants.

modification_rules.name: ii7 preferred over IVmaj7 in IV-V-I
modification_rules.summary: IVmaj7's root-to-7 major-7 interval is strident; ii7's minor-7 interval is mild. Engine signal: rank ii7 voicings higher than IVmaj7 voicings for the IV function.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Existing PR #237 ii7-over-IVmaj7-substitution principle, sourced from MCP.

systems.name: Inner-Voice Counterpoint (SATB Texture)
systems.summary: Greene's most distinctive contribution and the most relational of his systems. Melody stays on top; inner voices (alto, tenor) move stepwise between chord changes; bass anchors with chord roots or moves stepwise/by-fifth to produce a true counterpoint line. Unlike Pass's harmonic restraint, Greene's framework prefers to harmonize every melody note.

traversal_rules.name: Inner voices move stepwise between chord changes
traversal_rules.summary: Melody on top; inner voices (alto, tenor) move stepwise. Greene's contrapuntal default.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Chord Chemistry. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1971.
references.citation: Existing PR #237 voice-leading-inner-motion principle, sourced from Chord Chemistry.

traversal_rules.name: Bass anchors or walks as counterpoint
traversal_rules.summary: Bass voice anchors with chord roots or moves stepwise/by-fifth to produce a true counterpoint line.

references.source: Ted Greene lesson archive at masters-corpus/greene/
references.citation: Corpus: 1-to-1BaroqueCounterpoint_OrganizedOver3UnitBassLines_TedGreene_1984-01-14.txt.

traversal_rules.name: Common-tone preferred for soprano between consecutive voicings
traversal_rules.summary: When adjacent chords share a possible melody pitch, prefer that pitch — melody note is the 'fixed' voice.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Chord Chemistry. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1971.
references.citation: Greene's chord-melody pedagogy throughout.

traversal_rules.name: Sequential articulation: bass plucked first, inner voices in sequence, melody on top
traversal_rules.summary: Technique rule — affects voicing texture without changing voicing shape.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Existing PR #237 sequential-articulation principle + Greene's 1995 letter to Bruce.

modification_rules.name: Conversion between voicings preserves voice independence
modification_rules.summary: Greene's Conversion method (V-system R1.2) serves this rule — voicings should be reachable without breaking the multi-voice texture.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Chord Chemistry. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1971.
references.citation: Combined with V-system's Conversion table.

modification_rules.name: High NCT-harmonization appetite (harmonize aggressively)
modification_rules.summary: Greene's framework prefers to harmonize every melody note (or nearly every). Inverse of Pass's R4.2.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Chord Chemistry. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1971.
references.citation: Pervasive across Greene's pedagogy.

preferences.name: High NCT-harmonization appetite
preferences.summary: Greene harmonizes almost every melody note — the opposite extreme from Pass.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Chord Chemistry. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1971.
references.citation: Defining preference contrast with Pass.

systems.name: Symmetric Movement (Non-Functional Planing)
systems.summary: Non-functional chord movement by intervallic symmetry. Shifting all voices by the named interval moves to a new symmetric voicing — whole-tone planing produces 6-cycle voicings; minor-third 4-cycle; major-third 3-cycle; half-step is fully chromatic.

traversal_rules.name: Plane the chord shape by symmetric interval
traversal_rules.summary: Move all voices by the symmetric interval to produce the next voicing.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Chapter 6 'Moving Voices'.

modification_rules.name: Substitute symmetric-related voicing for written voicing
modification_rules.summary: Within a symmetric movement class, any of the symmetric-related voicings can substitute.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Chapter 6 worked examples.

modification_rules.name: Use symmetric planing to harmonize chromatic melody lines
modification_rules.summary: When melody moves by a symmetric interval, planing the voicing by that interval is a natural harmonization.

references.source: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions: Jazz and Classical Voicings for Guitar. Dale Zdenek Publications, 1976.
references.citation: Corpus material on planing as harmonization technique.

masters.name: Joe Pass
masters.biography: Bebop guitarist whose solo recordings (Virtuoso series, 1973-1983) reset expectations for unaccompanied jazz guitar. Combined walking bass lines with chord stabs and single-note runs from held chord positions.

principles.name: Walking bass plus chord stabs
principles.summary: Bass walks on every beat (chord tones, passing notes between chord tones, chord-extension notes, tritone approaches to the root); chord 'stabs' land on off-beats with 2-3 note voicings rather than full block chords. The texture blends 'walking bass, inner voices, and lead lines into a single texture.' Voicings must keep the bass string accessible.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Style
references.citation: Pass, Joe and Bill Thrasher. Joe Pass Guitar Style. Gwyn Publishing, 1971.

references.source: jazz-guitar-licks.com — Joe Pass walking-bass transcription
references.citation: Documents the walking-bass mechanics: chord tones + passing notes + chord-extension notes + tritone approaches.

principles.name: Drop-2 and drop-3 chord-melody harmonization
principles.summary: Pass's typical chord-melody technique harmonizes the melody with drop-2 and drop-3 voicings. Drop-2 keeps melody on top of a 4-note voicing on adjacent strings; drop-3 spreads the voicing wider with a 'skip' in the middle. The melody is on the top string of the voicing; the chord tones fill in below.

references.source: drdavewalkerblog — Joe Pass Solo Jazz Guitar
references.citation: Documents Pass's typical chord-melody technique as drop-2 and drop-3 harmonization.

principles.name: Harmonic restraint — don't reharmonize every note
principles.summary: Pass's published advice: 'You don't need a chord for every note' — add color through alterations rather than constant reharmonization. On ii-V-I, he often bypasses the ii ('forget the II — focus on the V'). The aesthetic privileges voice-leading economy over harmonic density. 'You don't have to play bass, chord, melody all the time — dip in and out between all three.'

references.source: Sam Blakelock — What Was Joe Pass's Approach to Solo Jazz Guitar?
references.citation: Quotes Pass: 'You don't need a chord for every note,' 'forget the II,' 'dip in and out between all three.'

principles.name: Single-note runs from chord shapes
principles.summary: Improvise melodically by playing single notes drawn from the chord shape currently held. The held shape acts as a position anchor; soloing fingers move between the chord tones without requiring a separate scale framework. Underpins his single-note-to-chord transitions in solo arrangements.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Style
references.citation: Pass and Thrasher. Joe Pass Guitar Style. Gwyn, 1971.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734), p. 4: 'With the Chord Forms acting as points of anchor or reference, the ability to create an improvised scale or melody will be unhampered by the struggle to find the notes.'

principles.name: Six Chord Forms as positional substrate
principles.summary: Pass's framework divides the fingerboard into six overlapping positional regions named for the chord shape that anchors each one — C, A, G, E, D, and Intermediate Forms — each spanning roughly five to six frets. Choosing a voicing is therefore also choosing a position: the player picks the Form whose chord shape lets the melody, bass, and inner voices sit inside one hand position. This is the spatial substrate that makes the walking-bass-plus-chord-stabs and runs-from-chord-shapes principles physically executable.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734), p. 4: 'Below are six diagrams which are usually learned by all guitarists when beginning to play in first position [...]. All scales are played vertically within each form whose object is to provide a framework in which to function.' The six forms enumerated are C, A, G, E, D (1st inversion of C), and Intermediate (top part of C).

principles.name: Three- to four-note voicings linked by voice-leading
principles.summary: For unaccompanied solo work Pass deliberately reduces voicings to three or four sounding notes and connects successive chords through common tones or stepwise inner-voice motion. Full six-string block chords are avoided as the default. The principle complements drop-2/drop-3 chord-melody — drop voicings supply the shape, this principle constrains the density and the linkage between adjacent chords. Engine signal: floor voicings at three sounding notes; rank candidates that share tones with the previous voicing higher.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734), p. 30, head note to 'Blues for Nina': 'All changes should be reduced to three- or four-note chords. And for better movement, voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them. Nobs contains several examples of this technique.' The 'EXAMPLES OF VOICE LEADING' panel on p. 31 demonstrates the principle with the sequence Dm9 → C13 → C9(b5) → C9(6)/C7 → Fmaj7.

principles.name: Dominant alteration and tritone substitution lattice
principles.summary: A written 'simple' dominant chord licenses an entire family of altered and extended substitutes — 9, 13, 9(6), 11, +9, b9, +5, b5 — plus the tritone substitute and its altered family. In the *Joe Pass Guitar Method* this is stated as an explicit rule for the whole 'Blues for Nina / Nobs / Alison / Grete' set: any of these alterations 'may be substituted for simple Dominants.' On Joe's Blues, plain G7 expands to G13, G9, G9(6), G7+9, G7(b9); E7 expands to E+9/Em9/E13/E9 or the tritone family Bb9/Bb+9/Bbm9/Bb13. Engine signal: when ranking voicings against a notated dominant, the altered/extended family and the tritone substitute should both be eligible candidates carrying Pass's style tag.

references.source: Joe Pass Guitar Method
references.citation: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co., 1977 (Hal Leonard HL00347734). 'Joe's Blues' head note, p. 25, enumerates substitutes for G7 (G13/G9/G9(6)/G7+9/G7(b9)), Bm7 in bar 8 (F13/F9/F+9/Fm9, or B7/B9/B13), and E7 (E+9/Em9/E13/E9 or the tritone-substitute family Bb9/Bb+9/Bbm9/Bb13/Bb9). 'Blues for Nina' head note, p. 30: 'Throughout, +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants.'

systems.name: Six Chord Forms (Position System)
systems.summary: Pass partitions the fretboard into six overlapping positional regions, each anchored on a chord shape: C, A, G, E, D, and Intermediate (top part of C Form). Each Form occupies ~5-6 frets, defines a scale-fingering window, and serves as the spatial reference for chord-melody and improvisation work. Position is identified by which Form anchors the hand; movement between Forms uses pivot-finger shifts.

members.name: C Form
members.summary: Anchor at C major shape; canonical pivot for Pass's chord-melody.

members.name: D Form
members.summary: First inversion of C Form.

members.name: Intermediate Form
members.summary: Top part of C Form; secondary.

traversal_rules.name: Pivot-finger shift to next Form
traversal_rules.summary: To move between Forms, use the 1st or 4th finger to play two consecutive notes; the second note lands the hand in the next Form.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: ch5-1, p. 19: 'one can shift to any string by using either the first finger or fourth finger to play two notes. This will place the hand on the next Chord Form.'

traversal_rules.name: Same-Form continuity preferred
traversal_rules.summary: While the musical context permits, stay in one Form across consecutive voicings to keep scale fingerings and melody-line landing positions consistent.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Implicit across pp. 4-21; explicit cross-ref ch8-3 p. 23: 'When playing a Dominant 7th Chord or Scale, one Chord Form must be kept in mind.'

preferences.name: Chord-form preference order
preferences.summary: Pass mentions A, E, C, D as the four core Forms; G and Intermediate are secondary. A and E come first in his enumeration.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 6: 'By mastering all the scales in the A — E — C/D Chord Forms, which are basically similar, the student will have covered the fingerboard quite thoroughly.'

systems.name: Three Harmonic Families (Major / Minor / Dominant)
systems.summary: Pass partitions all chord qualities into three families that share voicing-pool characteristics. Major covers maj6/maj7/maj9/maj13/6-9; Minor covers m6/m7/m9/m-maj7/m11; Dominant covers dom7/dom9/dom13 and ALL altered/extended dominants, with diminished and augmented as subspecies of Dominant. Within a family, voicing-level substitution is free (a written maj7 admits maj6, maj9, maj13, 6/9 with no harmonic re-analysis).

members.name: Major family
members.summary: Covers maj6, maj7, maj9, maj13, 6/9.

members.name: Minor family
members.summary: Covers m6, m7, m9, m-maj7, m11.

members.name: Dominant family
members.summary: Covers dom7/9/13 and all altered/extended dominants; diminished and augmented are subspecies.

traversal_rules.name: Family-coherent comping preferred
traversal_rules.summary: When traversing a chord progression, voicings drawn from the same family share recognition cues; mixing families is fine but is itself a compositional choice.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 16: 'the underlying chord sequence for this study should be analyzed, remembering that it can be broken down into three simple chord types: Major, Minor, and Dominant 7th (Aug./Dim.).'

modification_rules.name: Within-family substitution is free
modification_rules.summary: A written maj7 admits maj6, maj9, maj13, 6/9 as voicing-level substitutes — same family, same listener recognition.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 16 + cross-reference Complete Chord Melody Module 2 which credits this concept to Pass by name.

systems.name: Voicing Density and Voice Leading
systems.summary: Pass treats voicing-internal density and inter-voicing motion as coupled. Solo work runs on 3-4 sounding notes; walking-bass texture drops to 2-3-note stabs; 5-6-note block chords are the avoided default. Voice motion preferences are common-tone first, stepwise inner as fallback.

traversal_rules.name: Common-tone preferred, stepwise inner as fallback
traversal_rules.summary: Voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them; stepwise inner-voice motion is the fallback when no common tone is available.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30: 'voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them.' Worked example pp. 31-32 (Dm9 → C13 → C7(b5) → C9(6)/C7 → Fmaj7) demonstrates per-transition shared tones.

modification_rules.name: Density ceiling — 4 sounding notes in solo work
modification_rules.summary: Five- or six-note block chords are NOT Pass's default for solo work.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30: 'All changes should be reduced to three- or four-note chords.'

modification_rules.name: Density floor — 3 sounding notes in solo work
modification_rules.summary: Pass's solo arrangements use at least 3 sounding notes; bare 2-note voicings are walking-bass territory, not solo.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30 same passage: 'three- or four-note chords' establishes 3 as the floor for solo contexts.

modification_rules.name: Walking-bass texture with 2-note chord stabs
modification_rules.summary: Bass walks every beat; chord stabs land off-beats with 2-3-note voicings rather than full block chords.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Joe's Blues worked example pp. 25-29; corroborated by PR #239 walking-bass-plus-chord-stabs principle (originally from Joe Pass Guitar Style 1971).

modification_rules.name: Drop-2 and Drop-3 are the default chord-melody shapes
modification_rules.summary: Method's pp. 31-32 worked examples are predominantly drop-2 shapes; drop-3 is the secondary chord-melody shape.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: pp. 31-32 worked examples; explicit drop-2 / drop-3 terminology elsewhere in Pass's secondary literature.

preferences.name: Voice-motion preference order
preferences.summary: Pass's explicit preference: common-tone first, then stepwise inner, then leap, parallel last (avoided).

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30 same passage as R3.1.

systems.name: Harmonic Restraint (Three-Textures Cycling)
systems.summary: The question of whether to harmonize a given melody note at all. Pass cycles through three textures within one solo arrangement — harmonize, leave-as-single-note, dip-out-to-bass-only — weaving rather than maintaining a single texture. Color comes from chord alteration (System 5), not from harmonizing every note.

traversal_rules.name: Cycle between bass/chord/melody textures within an arrangement
traversal_rules.summary: The arrangement weaves between three textures rather than maintaining one. Per Pass's own framing: 'You don't have to play bass, chord, melody all the time — dip in and out between all three.'

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Secondary-source quote of Pass, corroborated by Method's improvisation chapters where single-note lines dominate page-count.

modification_rules.name: Don't harmonize every note (low NCT-harmonization appetite)
modification_rules.summary: Pass's restraint: 'You don't need a chord for every note.' Solo arrangements deliberately leave melodic NCT passages as bare single-note lines.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Sam Blakelock interview quoting Pass; corroborated by Method's improvisation chapters' page-count dominance of single-note lines over chord-density.

modification_rules.name: Bypass the ii in ii-V-I — focus on the V
modification_rules.summary: Pass's specific habit of leaving the ii bar as a single-note line and harmonizing only the V resolution. Functional shorthand: the V carries the ii's information.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Pass quote: 'Forget the II — focus on the V.' Secondary-source corroborated; Method's worked examples consistently apply this in ii-V passages.

preferences.name: Low NCT-harmonization appetite
preferences.summary: Lower than the bookshelf default; encodes Pass's restraint distinct from Greene's aggressive harmonization.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: Same Blakelock-quoted passage as R4.2.

systems.name: Dominant Substitution Lattice
systems.summary: Pass's richest system and the engine-active one. Any written dominant chord admits an expansion-set of related qualities (dom9, dom13, dom7+9, dom7b9, dom7+5, dom7b5, dom7b13, dom-alt). Tritone-related pairs and diminished-symmetry quartets group roots; color tones (b9, +9, b5, +5) are baseline-not-optional even when 'playing the written changes'.

members.name: Tritone-related root pair (C7 ↔ F#7)
members.summary: Twelve canonical pairs covering all roots.

members.name: Diminished-symmetry root quartet
members.summary: {D7, F7, Ab7, B7}, {Db7, E7, G7, Bb7}, {C7, Eb7, Gb7, A7} — three quartets cover all twelve.

traversal_rules.name: Tritone substitution carries color alteration through
traversal_rules.summary: E7 admits Bb9/Bb+9/Bbm9/Bb13 — same alteration palette on the tritone-related root. The symmetric-pair member carries the same color tones.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 25 (ch9-2): 'Bm7 can be replaced by F13/F9/F+9/Fm9. Bm7 can be changed to a Dominant 7th (B7) or B9/B13.'

modification_rules.name: Tune-wide alteration permission
modification_rules.summary: Throughout, +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants. This rule licenses substitution across the entire piece, not just one chord position.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 30 (ch10-4): 'Throughout, +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants.'

modification_rules.name: Color tones (b9, +9, b5, +5) are baseline in dom7 lines, not optional
modification_rules.summary: Even when playing the written changes, Pass includes a color tone (b9, +9, b5, or +5) in his dom7 lines as the baseline expectation, not as an embellishment.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: p. 9 (ch1-6): 'Against almost any Dominant 7th Chord, I will include the b9, +9, b5, or +5 in my lines.'

preferences.name: High substitution aggressiveness
preferences.summary: Pass's appetite for substitution is high — R5.3's 'throughout, may be substituted' + ch9-4 'Students must be able to choose freely from the alternatives' both signal aggressive expansion.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. 1977. Chappell Music / Hal Leonard HL00347734.
references.citation: pp. 25, 30; ch9-4 'choose freely from the alternatives'.

works.title: The Joe Pass Guitar Method
