works.summary: Joe Pass guitar method published 1977 by Chappell. 34-page method organizing scalar and harmonic instruction around the Chord Forms (A, E, C, D, G, Intermediate) as fingerboard navigational anchors. Covers scale-to-chord pairing rules (diminished scales over Dom7; whole-tone scales over Dom7+5), dominant flexibility and tritone substitution, voicing economy (3-4 note reduction with stepwise or common-tone voice leading), and four composition studies (Joe's Blues, Blues for Nina, NOBS-Alison-Grete) as applied laboratories. Ear-verification — that learned material must pass through the ear before application — is the non-negotiable governor throughout.

references.source: Pass, Joe. The Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chappell & Co. (1977). 34 pages.
references.citation: Primary source extracted via #297 master-distillation pipeline; promoted under #347 batch-1.

systems.name: Chord Forms Navigation System
systems.summary: The central navigational system of the Joe Pass Guitar Method. Chord Forms (A, E, C, D, G, and Intermediate) serve as vertical anchors on the fingerboard, providing a framework within which scalar and melodic material becomes automatic. The system defines six Form members as position-regions, specifies how the player moves between them via pivot-finger shifts (1st or 4th finger), mandates practice across all Forms in both chromatic and Form order, and requires every scale-line exercise to begin and end with the parent chord voicing so that melody is always tethered to harmony. Mastery of A–E–C/D Forms is prioritized as the most efficient path to full fingerboard coverage. The ear-verification rule — that learned patterns must pass through the ear before application — is the non-negotiable governor of the entire system.

members.name: A Chord Form
members.summary: One of the three primary Forms covering the fingerboard thoroughly.

members.name: E Chord Form
members.summary: One of the three primary Forms; structurally similar to A Form.

members.name: C Chord Form
members.summary: Primary Form; related to D Form.

members.name: D Chord Form
members.summary: Structurally equivalent to C Form at a different register.

members.name: G Chord Form
members.summary: Contains note-choice decisions (B on d or g string).

members.name: Intermediate Chord Form
members.summary: Sixth Form; scale practice exception (no root-to-root requirement).

traversal_rules.name: Pivot-Finger Position Shift
traversal_rules.summary: Use the 1st or 4th finger to play two notes when shifting from one Chord Form position to the next.

references.quote_excerpt: shift to any string by using either the first finger or fourth finger to play two notes

traversal_rules.name: Form-Order and Chromatic-Order Practice Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Traverse all Forms either in chromatic key order within a single Form, or in Form order (C–A–G–E–Intermediate–D) for a given key, covering two octaves per scale.

references.quote_excerpt: practice in all Forms either chromatically or in Form order

traversal_rules.name: Chord-Frame Scale-Line Traversal
traversal_rules.summary: Every scale-line exercise must begin and end with the parent chord voicing, anchoring melodic traversal to its harmonic origin.

references.quote_excerpt: chords on which these scale lines are based should be played at the beginning and ending of each exercise

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic Half-Step Neck Extension
traversal_rules.summary: Extend chromatic patterns up the neck by half-steps, moving through successive Form positions.

references.quote_excerpt: starting with open E, then moving up by half-steps to extend the exercise

modification_rules.name: Scale Extrapolation Across All Forms
modification_rules.summary: Any scale given in abbreviated form must be repeated by the student in all six Forms with the same fingering.

references.quote_excerpt: These scales should be repeated in all Forms; the fingering is the same

modification_rules.name: A–E–C/D Form Priority Rule
modification_rules.summary: Prioritize mastery of A, E, and C/D Forms first; together they cover the fingerboard thoroughly.

references.quote_excerpt: By mastering all the scales in the A — E — C/D Chord Forms, which are basically similar, the student will have covered the fingerboard quite thoroughly

modification_rules.name: Ear-Verification Rule
modification_rules.summary: All learned material must pass through the ear before being applied; mechanical application without listening is prohibited.

references.quote_excerpt: the student is cautioned to use his ears

references.topic: Music from the Head, Not Fingers
references.quote_excerpt: music must come from the head, not simply from the fingers running up and down the neck

modification_rules.name: Dominant 7th Flat-Seventh Form Rule
modification_rules.summary: When improvising on a Dominant 7th, anchor to the Basic Chord Form and simply flat the seventh tone to reduce mistakes.

references.quote_excerpt: By constantly using and thinking in terms of the Basic Chord Forms and simply flatting the seventh tone

systems.name: Dominant Flexibility and Substitution System
systems.summary: This system governs how the player selects, alters, and substitutes dominant chords across the blues and jazz repertoire taught in the method. Its members are the dominant chord quality families: plain dominant 7th, extended dominants, altered dominants, augmented dominant, tritone substitute family, and compound altered dominants. Traversal rules describe scale-to-dominant pairings (diminished scales for dom7, whole-tone scales for dom7+5). Modification rules define interchangeability of extended and altered dominants with plain dominants, tritone substitution (E7 ↔ Bb9 family), and compound altered dominants for chromatic voice movement. Free ear-driven choice among available substitutions is the governing principle.

members.name: Plain Dominant 7th
members.summary: Base dominant chord; e.g. G7, C7, D7.

members.name: Extended Dominants
members.summary: Dominant chords with added 9/11/13/9(6); e.g. G9, G13, G11.

members.name: Altered Dominants
members.summary: Dominant chords with b5, +5, +9, b9; e.g. G7(+9), G7(b9).

members.name: Augmented Dominant (Dom7+5)
members.summary: Dominant chord exclusively paired with whole-tone scale.

members.name: Tritone Substitute Family
members.summary: Chords a tritone away from the target dominant; e.g. Bb9 family for E7.

members.name: Compound Altered Dominant
members.summary: Combined altered extensions for chromatic resolution; e.g. G7(+5/b9) to C9(6).

traversal_rules.name: Diminished Scale Exclusive Dom7 Pairing
traversal_rules.summary: The three diminished scales are played exclusively against Dominant 7th chords.

references.quote_excerpt: are all to be played against Dominant 7th Chords

traversal_rules.name: Whole-Tone Scale Dom7+5 Pairing
traversal_rules.summary: The two whole-tone scales fit exclusively with Dominant 7+5 chords.

references.quote_excerpt: There are but two Whole Tone Scales. They will fit with all Dominant 7 +5 Chords

traversal_rules.name: Chord-Derived Melodic Line Generation
traversal_rules.summary: Melodic patterns grow out of the chord at the start of each line; lines are chord-first, not scale-first.

references.quote_excerpt: melodic/scale patterns, in the following examples, grow out of the chords at the beginning of each line

modification_rules.name: Extended/Altered Dominant Free Substitution
modification_rules.summary: Any of +9, b9, 13, 9(6), 11, +5, b5 extensions may be freely substituted for a plain dominant chord.

references.quote_excerpt: +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/-+5/b5 may be substituted for simple Dominants

modification_rules.name: Tritone Substitution Rule
modification_rules.summary: E7 and the Bb9 family are interchangeable; tritone-equivalent dominants substitute freely.

references.quote_excerpt: E7 can be replaced with E+9/Em9/E13/E9 or Bb 9/Bb+9/Bb m9/Bb13/Bb9

modification_rules.name: Tonic Dominant Color Variant Palette
modification_rules.summary: For the tonic dominant (G7), any of G13, G9, G9(6), G7+9, G7(b9) may be used as color substitutions.

references.quote_excerpt: variants may be used in place of G7: G13, G9, G9(6), G7 +9, G7(b9)

modification_rules.name: Compound Altered Dominant Chromatic Resolution
modification_rules.summary: Compound altered dominants (G7+5/b9) resolve to extended tonics (C9(6)) for chromatic voice movement.

references.quote_excerpt: G7(+5/b9) —-C9(6) (c) Fmaj7 voice leading

modification_rules.name: Turnaround Bar Pivot Substitution
modification_rules.summary: In bar 8, Bm7 may be replaced with F-family dominants or converted to B7/B9/B13 in the second half of the bar.

references.quote_excerpt: In bar 8 Bm7 can be replaced by F13/F9/F +9/Fm9

modification_rules.name: Ear-Driven Harmonic Choice Verification
modification_rules.summary: Students freely choose from available alternatives and verify each improvised phrase against the chord they have chosen.

references.topic: Free choice in chording and improvisation
references.quote_excerpt: Students must be able to choose freely from the alternatives available

systems.name: Voicing Economy and Voice-Leading System
systems.summary: This system governs how chords are voiced and connected across a progression. Members are voicing-density categories (three-note, four-note, thick/rejected) and the harmonic-function chord types (tonic, subdominant, supertonic ii, dominant V, substituted). Two complementary rules govern movement: voicings must connect by stepwise voice motion or share a common tone, and all chord changes must be reduced to three or four notes. Demonstrated in NOBS (Ch. 10) and applied in Alison (Ch. 11) where F13, Bbm7, and Ebmaj7 substitute into the C7–F7–Bbmaj7–Eb backbone, and the ii–V–I with altered dominant extensions (Dm7–G7(13)/G7(+5)–Cmaj7) serves as the core template.

members.name: Three-Note Voicing
members.summary: Minimum density; preferred economy voicing.

members.name: Four-Note Voicing
members.summary: Maximum permitted density under the economy rule.

members.name: Thick/Dense Voicing (Rejected)
members.summary: Voicings exceeding four notes; explicitly prohibited.

members.name: Tonic Chord (I)
members.summary: Root-function chord; e.g. Cmaj7, Bbmaj7, C9(6).

members.name: Subdominant Chord (IV)
members.summary: Fourth-function chord; e.g. F7, Bbmaj7, Ebmaj7.

members.name: Supertonic Chord (ii)
members.summary: Second-function chord; e.g. Dm7, Dm9.

members.name: Dominant Chord (V)
members.summary: Fifth-function chord including altered forms.

members.name: Substituted Harmony
members.summary: Chord substituting for a functional root; e.g. F13 over C7.

traversal_rules.name: Stepwise Motion or Common-Tone Connection
traversal_rules.summary: Voicings must either lead into one another through stepwise motion or share a common tone.

references.quote_excerpt: voicings should lead into one another or have a common tone connecting them

traversal_rules.name: ii–V–I Altered Dominant Voice-Leading Template
traversal_rules.summary: The ii–V–I with altered dominant extensions is the core voice-leading template.

references.quote_excerpt: Dm7 G7(13) G7(+5) Cmaj7 or Cm9 voice leading

traversal_rules.name: Chord-Voicing Substitution Over Backbone
traversal_rules.summary: Substituted voicings move over the underlying functional backbone (C7–F7–Bbmaj7–Eb).

references.quote_excerpt: (C7) (F7) (Bbmaj7) (Eb)

modification_rules.name: Four-Note Density Ceiling
modification_rules.summary: All chord changes must be reduced to three or four notes; no voicing may exceed this density.

references.quote_excerpt: All changes should be reduced to three- or four-note chords

modification_rules.name: Altered Dominant Color-Tone Requirement for Voice Leading
modification_rules.summary: In the ii–V–I template, the dominant must carry at least one altered extension to generate chromatic voice motion.

masters.name: Martin Taylor
masters.biography: Scottish virtuoso whose solo fingerstyle approach extends the Django Reinhardt and Joe Pass traditions with greater fluidity in thumb-bass / chord / melody independence. Played a decade with Stéphane Grappelli; authored Solo Jazz Guitar (Mel Bay), Single Note Soloing (Mel Bay), the Fingerstyle Jazz Guitar DVD with Grossman's Guitar Workshop, and runs the Martin Taylor Guitar Academy with extensive online lessons. Cites Bill Evans and Art Tatum as keyboard influences for melody-bass separation.

principles.name: Alternating thumb-bass strokes
principles.summary: The right-hand thumb alternates between the 6th and 5th strings (or 6th/4th depending on chord) to maintain continuous bass support without interrupting melodic flow. Demonstrated in Taylor's '4 Bar 4 Shapes' etude: thumb keeps a walking pulse while the fingers play melody and chord stabs above. The thumb is mechanically independent of the fingers — neither waits for the other.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor (guitarist)
references.citation: Documents 'alternating thumb strokes between the 6th and 5th strings to maintain rhythmic drive, as demonstrated in etudes like 4 Bar 4 Shapes.'

principles.name: Melody-bass separation (Tatum/Evans-derived)
principles.summary: Clear textural separation between melody (top voice) and bass (thumb), with chord work as a third middle layer. Taylor cites Bill Evans and Art Tatum as keyboard influences for this approach — the goal is to sound like two players, not one. Distinguishes itself from Django by greater independence between layers rather than Django's parallel-voicings style.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor
references.citation: 'A clear melody bass separation and crisp chordal sophistication inspired by his keyboard heroes, Bill Evans and Art Tatum.'

principles.name: Internal dynamics across voices
principles.summary: Taylor's own term: 'internal dynamics' — don't treat every note the same. Bass gets a specific touch (often softer, percussive); melody comes out louder; chord stabs sit in the middle. Voicings are selected partly for whether they support this dynamic stratification — voicings that put bass on a louder open string aren't useful if the bass needs to recede.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor
references.citation: 'When playing fingerstyle guitar, he doesn't treat every note the same, giving them different sound textures and balance.'

principles.name: Natural and artificial harmonics in arrangement
principles.summary: Taylor incorporates both natural harmonics (open strings at fret-12 / fret-7 nodes) and artificial harmonics (right-hand harp-harmonic technique, related to Chet Atkins / Lenny Breau) as melodic colors. Voicings designed to terminate on or transition to a harmonic exploit the upper-string register that fretted notes can't access.

references.source: Grokipedia — Martin Taylor
references.citation: 'Right-hand fingerstyle techniques, natural and artificial harmonics, walking bass lines, double thumbing bass lines, single note improvisation, playing melodies in and out of tempo.'

systems.name: Alternating-Thumb Bass (Patterned)
systems.summary: Right-hand thumb plays bass in alternating-string pattern (boom-chick-style, Reinhardt + folk-fingerstyle derived). Distinct from Breau's three-voice (Taylor's is more rhythmically regular) and Pass's walking-bass (Taylor's is patterned, not improvised).

members.name: Root + 5th alternation on adjacent low strings
Thumb walks in passing-tone contexts (borrows from Pass)

traversal_rules.name: Thumb alternates between root and 5th of the chord
traversal_rules.summary: Boom-chick pattern on adjacent low strings.

systems.name: Strict Melody-Bass Separation
systems.summary: Tatum/Bill-Evans-derived pianistic split — melody on treble strings (fingers), bass on bass strings (thumb), chord stabs fill the middle. Melody and bass move independently in time AND direction; contrary motion common.

traversal_rules.summary: Contrary motion is common.

systems.summary: Dynamic variation BETWEEN voices in the same voicing — bass louder, melody softer, chord stabs medium. Internal dynamic profile of a voicing is itself a compositional choice. Likely a playback affordance rather than a voicing selector.

systems.summary: Natural + artificial harmonics as melodic resource. Used for chord coloring and melodic statements at structural points (intros, codas, melodic peaks) — not just timbral decoration.

modification_rules.summary: Related to Breau's harp-harmonics — possibly subsumed under one harmonics-vocabulary payload.

masters.name: Wes Montgomery
masters.biography: Self-taught Indianapolis guitarist whose innovations after 1959 reshaped jazz guitar: thumb-attack right hand, parallel-octave melodic statements, and block-chord chorus climaxes built directly from melodic shape. Active recording career 1959-1968 (Riverside, Verve, A&M) with canonical statements on Smokin' at the Half Note (1965) and The Incredible Jazz Guitar of Wes Montgomery (1960). No instructional book of his own; documentation is via transcription collections and biographical studies.

principles.name: Three-tier chorus development
principles.summary: A solo arcs through three distinct textural tiers: single-note lines (statement), parallel octaves (intensification), block-chord harmonization (climax). Each tier carries the same melodic logic; the chord tier is melody-led, not chord-led — the top voice of the chord IS the melody, with chord tones filled in below. The arc is documented across nearly every extended Wes solo from 'West Coast Blues' onward.

references.source: The Wes Montgomery Guitar Folio (Hal Leonard)
references.citation: Montgomery, Wes. The Wes Montgomery Guitar Folio. Hal Leonard, posthumous transcriptions.

references.source: Adrian Ingram, Wes Montgomery (Centerstream)
references.citation: Ingram, Adrian. Wes Montgomery. Centerstream Publications, 2008. Biographical study with technique analysis.

references.source: Smokin' at the Half Note (1965)
references.citation: Recorded reference: 'Four on Six,' 'No Blues' — canonical three-tier choruses.

principles.name: Octave-passage construction
principles.summary: Parallel octaves played between non-adjacent strings — strings 6 and 4, 5 and 3, 4 and 2, or 3 and 1 depending on position. The thumb mutes the intervening string while striking both target strings. Octave passages are melodically motivated lines, not just doubled single-note lines: pitch contour and rhythm match the surrounding tier's logic.

references.source: Steve Khan, Pat Martino: The Nature of Guitar (analysis chapter on Wes)
references.citation: Khan, Steve. Analyses across multiple Wes solo transcriptions. The thumb-muting mechanism is documented technique.

principles.name: Block-chord drop-2 predominance
principles.summary: Wes's block-chord soloing uses drop-2 voicings as the primary vehicle — voicings on adjacent strings with the second-from-top voice dropped an octave. The melody lives on the top string of the voicing; chord tones fill in below. Voice-leading between adjacent block chords is typically smooth in the top voice with parallel inner motion.

references.source: Voicelid Jazz Guitar — Drop-2 + Upper Structures
references.citation: Documents drop-2 as 'the vehicle for these ideas' and notes Wes 'stuck with chords played mainly on adjacent strings and drop 2 chord shapes.'

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Wes Montgomery chord soloing
references.citation: Indexed across the 40-licks article and Wes Montgomery chord-solo lesson.

principles.name: Thumb-attack articulation
principles.summary: Right-hand technique uses the thumb only — no plectrum and no multi-finger fingerstyle. The thumb downstrokes single notes, brushes adjacent strings for octaves, and rakes across the chord for block voicings. The resulting tone is warmer and less defined than a pick attack; the constraint also influences voicing choice — block chords with the bass on the lower strings are thumb-friendly, while sweeps across all six strings are not.

references.source: Adrian Ingram, Wes Montgomery (Centerstream)
references.citation: Ingram chapter on right-hand technique — the calluses on Wes's thumb are documented in interviews.

principles.name: Upper-structure triad substitution
principles.summary: Over a ii7-V7 (or single dominant), Wes voices an upper-structure chord that omits the root: e.g., a DbMaj7 shape over Bbm7 (the 3-5-9-11), then the same DbMaj7 shape carries forward over the following Eb7 (becoming b7-9-sus4-13). Wes treats the entire ii-V interaction as a unified 'Db major field,' reducing cognitive load while generating sophisticated harmonic color. Lines and chord stabs both draw from this field — single-note phrases imply complex harmony.

references.source: Voicelid Jazz Guitar — Drop-2 + Upper Structures
references.citation: Documents Wes's chord-over-chord substitution: 'his lines imply complex harmony even when he plays single notes.'

references.source: Wikipedia — Wes Montgomery
references.citation: Documents 'superimposed triads and arpeggios as the main source for his soloing ideas.'

systems.name: Three-Tier Chorus Architecture
systems.summary: Wes's canonical solo arc spans multiple choruses with a one-directional textural climb: tier-1 single-notes → tier-2 octaves → tier-3 block chords. Each tier reuses the melodic shape established in tier-1.

traversal_rules.name: Climb is monotonic across choruses
traversal_rules.summary: Once a higher tier is reached, return to a lower tier is uncommon.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #227 three-tier-chorus-development principle.

modification_rules.name: Section-aggregate density escalation
modification_rules.summary: Each successive chorus uses denser texture (1 → 2 → 4 sounding notes).

references.citation: Tier-by-tier density progression.

systems.name: Octave-Doubled Melody
systems.summary: Single melody note becomes two notes an octave apart, played together. Thumb-led with lower note often slightly behind the upper. Octave passages move parallel (no internal voice-leading).

members.name: Octave on string set 5-3 (root on 5, octave on 3)
Chromatic octave approach to chord tone

traversal_rules.name: Octave passages move parallel
traversal_rules.summary: Both notes move together; no internal voice-leading.

modification_rules.summary: Voicing pool restricts to 2-note octave voicings when tier-2 active.

systems.name: Block Chord Drop-2
systems.summary: Third-tier texture: 4-note block chords using drop-2 voicings. Top voice carries the melody (chord-melody texture). Consecutive block chords tend to stay on one string set across a phrase.

traversal_rules.name: Consecutive block chords share string-set
traversal_rules.summary: Block-chord passages stay on one string set; move by position rather than transference.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Wes's tier-3 articulation across recordings.

modification_rules.summary: Engine ranks category 'drop-2' voicings higher than other 4-note shapes when tier-3 active.

references.citation: PR #227 block-chord-drop-2-predominance principle.

systems.name: Thumb-Attack Articulation
systems.summary: Wes used his thumb (not a pick) as the primary attack, giving his sound its characteristic warm/round timbre. Every attack is a thumb downstroke. Limits maximum tempo; influences rhythmic feel.

traversal_rules.name: Downstroke-only with thumb
traversal_rules.summary: No alternate picking; every attack is a thumb downstroke.

references.source: plans/wes-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)

systems.name: Upper-Structure Triad Substitution Over Altered Dominants
systems.summary: Substitution over altered dominants via superimposed triads (e.g., Db major triad over C7 produces C7b9b13; D triad produces C7#11; Eb triad produces C7#9; F# triad gives a fully altered C7). The substitute is a TRIAD positioned above an implied dominant root — structurally distinct from chord-quality substitution.

members.name: Db triad over C7 → b9, b13
F# triad over C7 → fully altered

masters.name: Lenny Breau
masters.biography: Canadian guitarist (1941-1984) who synthesized jazz, country, flamenco, folk, and classical idioms. Renowned for right-hand finger independence — bass, chord, and melody as three simultaneously moving voices — and for taking Chet Atkins's harp-harmonic technique to unprecedented levels. Later in his career played a custom 7-string with a high A tuned two octaves above the 5th string. Cited as a primary influence by Bill Frisell, Pat Metheny, and many others.

principles.name: Three independent voices on six strings
principles.summary: Bass, chord, and melody played simultaneously via fingerstyle, with right-hand finger independence treating each voice on its own timeline. Breau implied 'two guitarists playing together at times' (Guitar World) — left-hand fretting and right-hand articulation both required mechanical independence between thumb (bass) and fingers (chord + melody). The chord voice is often 2-3 notes (not a full block) to leave room for the bass and melody.

references.source: Premier Guitar — How Did Lenny Breau Do That?
references.citation: Documents the right-hand independence technique.

references.source: Guitar World — Lenny Breau: The Forgotten Guitar Genius
references.citation: 'Using fingers or pairs of fingers on his right hand independently of each other to juggle bass, melody and chords... He really did create the impression of two guitarists playing together, at times.'

principles.name: Harp-harmonics cascades (Atkins-derived)
principles.summary: Artificial-harmonic technique developed by Chet Atkins and brought to a higher level by Breau. Right hand: thumb or index finger touches a node 12 frets above the fretted position while another finger (typically ring) plucks the string, producing a harmonic at that fretted note's pitch. Cascading between fretted notes and harmonics creates harp-like textures with notes ringing across non-adjacent strings.

references.source: Guitar World — Dale Turner on Lenny Breau's harp harmonics
references.citation: Documents the technique's Atkins origin and Breau's extension: 'sheets of cascading harmonics to create complex harmonies and melodies.'

references.source: Premier Guitar — How Did Lenny Breau Do That?
references.citation: 'After mastering the harp-harmonic technique developed by Atkins, Breau took it to previously unimagined levels.'

principles.name: Implied harmony from two-note bass
principles.summary: Breau's signature device: imply a full chord from just two low-register notes (often root + 7th, or root + 3rd, or 3rd + 7th — the guide tones), leaving the upper strings free for melody. The voicing is minimal in the bass register but the implication is rich — the listener fills in the missing voices. Predates and underpins his three-voice approach when bass is held to two notes only.

references.source: Guitar World — Lenny Breau: The Forgotten Guitar Genius
references.citation: 'Lenny's ability to imply harmony with two low register notes while playing melody on the upper strings was unprecedented.'

principles.name: 7-string with high A (later career)
principles.summary: Late in his career, Breau played a custom 7-string with a high A tuned two octaves above the 5th string. The high A extends melody range above the 1st-string E without retuning, allowing piano-like simultaneous bass + chord + high melody — Breau's stated goal was to 'replicate on guitar a pianist's capacity for simultaneous linear and chordal development.' Distinct from Van Eps's 7-string (low A); Breau adds at the TOP, Van Eps at the bottom.

references.source: Wikipedia — Lenny Breau
references.citation: 'A custom seven-string guitar equipped with a high A tuned two octaves above the fifth string, which allowed him to replicate on guitar a pianist's capacity for simultaneous linear and chordal development.'

systems.name: Three-Voice Independence
systems.summary: Three simultaneously-moving lines on 6 strings — thumb plays bass independently while fingers play chord stabs and melody. Each voice has its own rhythmic identity. Distinct from Greene's SATB four-voice and Van Eps's outer/inner four-voice.

traversal_rules.name: Bass walks while chord pulses while melody sings
traversal_rules.summary: Each voice has its own rhythmic identity.

references.source: plans/breau-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)

systems.name: Harp-Harmonics Cascades
systems.summary: Right hand plucks a string while right-hand index/middle taps a node 12 frets above, producing artificial harmonic in cascading patterns. Atkins-derived technique. Used for chord-melody where timbre suits (slow ballads, intros).

systems.name: Implied Harmony via Bass
systems.summary: 2-note voicings (bass + melody only) where inner harmony is IMPLIED rather than played. Bass-led variant of Hall's space-doctrine. Bass note suggests harmonic root; melody fills out the implied chord-tone color.

traversal_rules.name: Bass note suggests harmonic root; melody fills chord-tone color
traversal_rules.summary: The listener completes the harmony.

modification_rules.summary: Sparse-texture preference.

systems.name: 7-String High-A Melody Range
systems.summary: 7-string with HIGH A (A4, above standard e) — distinct from Van Eps's LOW A (below E). Adds a melody-range string above the standard sound box. Melody runs on high-A while standard 6-string territory handles bass + chord.

modification_rules.summary: Tuning-specific affordance for the high-A configuration.

masters.name: Jim Hall
masters.biography: Influential jazz comper and soloist (1930-2013) whose voicings emphasize space, fourths, and conversational interaction. Studied composition and arrangement formally; recordings with Bill Evans, Sonny Rollins, Paul Desmond, and Art Farmer defined a quieter, more dialogic approach to jazz guitar accompaniment. Authored Exploring Jazz Guitar (Hal Leonard) and Jazz Guitar Environments (1994).

principles.name: Guide-tone (3 and 7) voicings
principles.summary: Hall's comping foundation: 3rd and 7th of the chord on the middle strings, often as two-note voicings or grace-noted into fuller shapes. Guide tones imply the harmony's quality (major/minor/dominant) and resolve smoothly between adjacent chords with minimal motion. Foundation for the rest of the Hall vocabulary — fuller voicings layer above the guide tones.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: 'Hall uses 3rd and 7ths (guide tone) voicings and octaves for comping.'

references.source: Guitar World — How Jim Hall revolutionized modern jazz guitar
references.citation: Documents the guide-tone approach as Hall's foundation.

principles.name: Stacked fourths and open voicings
principles.summary: Hall's signature 'fourths' sound: voicings built from stacked perfect fourths rather than triadic thirds — beautiful and harmonically ambiguous, doesn't commit to a single tonality. Often complemented with bass notes for grounding and counter-melodies on top. Hall avoids root-position triads; open voicings with wide intervals between adjacent voices keep the register wide.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: Documents the fourths voicings + open triads/inversions, avoiding 'root-position sound.'

references.source: Jim Hall: Exploring Jazz Guitar
references.citation: Hall, Jim. Exploring Jazz Guitar. Hal Leonard.

principles.name: Space and implied harmony
principles.summary: Hall's comping deliberately leaves space — fewer notes per voicing (often 2-3), strategic silences. The voicing's job is to imply the chord, not exhaustively spell it. This works as 'a second part in the arrangement rather than just filling harmonic space' (jazzguitar.be) — the comp is a melodic line as much as a harmonic support.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: 'His comping serves as a second part in the arrangement rather than just filling harmonic space.'

principles.name: Small motifs with melody on top of comp
principles.summary: Even in comping, Hall plays small melodic motifs on the top strings above the guide-tone middle voices. The result is a layered texture: harmonic support in the middle, conversational melody on top. Voicings are chosen to free up the top 1-2 strings for this motivic melody.

references.source: jazzguitar.be — Jim Hall's chord comping
references.citation: 'Simple motifs, guide tone voicings in the middle strings with small melodies on the top strings.'

systems.name: Guide-Tone Comping (3-7 Voicings)
systems.summary: 2-note voicings of just the 3rd and 7th of the chord; rarely root. For dom7 = {3, b7}; for maj7 = {3, 7}; for m7 = {b3, b7}. Voice-leading by half-step (canonical 7→3 resolution on V7→I).

traversal_rules.name: Half-step 7→3 voice-leading on V→I
traversal_rules.summary: When chord changes, guide-tone voicing moves to the new chord's 3 and 7, often via half-step.

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Canonical V7→I guide-tone resolution.

modification_rules.name: Substitute 3-7 voicing for any standard 4-note voicing in comping
modification_rules.summary: Rank 2-note voicings high under context: comping or ensemble.

references.source: plans/hall-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: PR #237 guide-tone-comping principle.

systems.name: Quartal and Open Voicings
systems.summary: Stacks of fourths (quartal) and open voicings with large intervals between voices (often a 10th between bass and next voice). Quartal voicings move in parallel; substitute for traditional drop-2/drop-3 in modal contexts.

members.name: Open voicing with 10th between bass and next voice

systems.summary: Voicings are NOT placed on every available beat. Hall's signature is the implied harmony in the gaps. Per-beat probability of voicing decreases under Hall-active.

systems.summary: Short melodic statements (2-4 note motifs) with chord support below. Motivic development typical of Hall's compositional formal training.

masters.name: Johnny Smith
masters.biography: Master of close-voiced chord-melody (1922-2013). His 1952 'Moonlight in Vermont' (Royal Roost) became a template for the genre — tight closed voicings that 'are a finger stretch on guitar' and 'pianistic' in feel. Authored the Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar (Mel Bay). His playing combines closed-position chord voicings with 'rapidly ascending lines... more diatonic than chromatically-based' (Django-like but diatonic).

principles.name: Tight closed voicings
principles.summary: Closely-voiced chord-melody — all four voices within an octave, often within a 4-fret span. Produces a unified vertical sound rather than a wide-spread texture. The voicings are 'a finger stretch on guitar' precisely because pianistic close-position spelling doesn't lay out under-the-hand on a guitar fretboard; Smith made them work through deliberate left-hand mechanics.

references.source: Guitar Player — Vinyl Treasures: Moonlight in Vermont
references.citation: 'Smith's version of Moonlight in Vermont was known because of its tight closed voicings, which are a finger stretch on guitar.'

references.source: Mel Bay's Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar
references.citation: Smith, Johnny. Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar. Mel Bay.

principles.name: Pianistic wide-stretch chord voicings
principles.summary: Smith's voicings cover 'a large section of real estate on the guitar neck' — his other signature alongside close voicings is the wide-stretch chord where adjacent voices are placed across non-adjacent strings or wider fret spans for piano-like sonority. The two approaches (tight closed AND wide stretch) coexist; arrangements move between them depending on which sonority the melody calls for.

references.source: Guitar Player — Moonlight in Vermont feature
references.citation: 'His challenging wide-stretch chord voicings are very pianistic... covering a large section of real estate on the guitar neck.'

principles.name: Diatonic single-note lines (Django-derived but diatonic)
principles.summary: Smith's single-note playing inherits Django Reinhardt's rapid-ascending-line aesthetic but stays more diatonically rooted, less chromatically-driven than bebop vocabulary. The diatonicism makes the lines blend smoothly into the close-voiced chord-melody context — chromatic interpolations would clash with the close diatonic stack.

references.source: Wikipedia — Johnny Smith
references.citation: 'Closed-position chord voicings and rapidly ascending lines (reminiscent of Django Reinhardt, but more diatonic than chromatically-based).'

systems.name: Close Voicing with Stretch Tolerance
systems.summary: 4-note voicings spanning ≤5 frets, all voices within an octave or close. The defining Smith texture. Stretch tolerance is HIGHER than most masters — close voicings that require larger finger stretches are accepted under Smith-active where they'd be filtered out for others.

traversal_rules.name: Consecutive close voicings move stepwise
traversal_rules.summary: Minimal hand motion; voicings nest within a 3-fret span across a phrase.

systems.name: Pianistic Wide-Stretch Voicings
systems.summary: Voicings with internal intervals exceeding an octave. Smith's 'pianistic' voicings. Appear at melodic peaks and resolutions — register-conditional, not comping default.

modification_rules.name: Substitute wide-stretch when melody hits high register
modification_rules.summary: Register-conditional preference — when current register is high, wide-stretch preferred; otherwise close-voicing.

systems.name: Diatonic Line Vocabulary
systems.summary: Single-note lines drawn from diatonic chord-scales. Stays IN the key. Django-influenced rhythmic feel. Chromatic approach is rare (unlike Pass).

masters.name: Jody Fisher
masters.biography: Educator (1957-) whose Alfred Music series — Complete Jazz Guitar Method (Beginning / Intermediate / Mastering), Jazz Guitar Harmony, Chord Tone Soloing — systematized jazz guitar study for generations of self-taught players. Fisher's contribution is pedagogical structure rather than distinctive personal style: he codified the standard voicing vocabulary (drop-2, drop-3, extended harmonies, altered chords) into an ordered curriculum and provided 'hundreds of chord voicings and improvisation ideas' across the series.

principles.name: Pedagogical voicing taxonomy
principles.summary: Fisher categorizes voicings systematically for teaching: drop-2 (drop the second-from-top from a close-position stack), drop-3 (drop the third-from-top), abbreviated voicings, extended-harmony voicings (9ths/11ths/13ths), altered-chord formulas. The taxonomy isn't novel — it's the standard pedagogical framework — but Fisher's curriculum-builder organization is what makes it teachable to self-learners.

references.source: Jazz Guitar Harmony (Alfred Music)
references.citation: 'Chord scales, abbreviated voicings, extended harmonies, altered chords, substitutions, ii-V-I progressions, the dominant cycle.'

references.source: Complete Jazz Guitar Method: Intermediate (Alfred Music)
references.citation: 'ii-V-I progression, solo lines, altered chord formulas, and comping in various jazz feels.'

principles.name: Etude- and song-driven practice methodology
principles.summary: Fisher's pedagogical structure pairs every new concept (chord type, voicing family, substitution rule) with concrete etudes AND songs that exercise it. The principle for voicing-selection: a voicing is properly learned when it's been used in BOTH an abstract exercise (etude) and a musical context (song). Carries into the plugin domain as a preference for voicings that the user has explicit musical context for, not just position-tabular acquaintance.

references.source: Complete Jazz Guitar Method: Intermediate (Alfred Music)
references.citation: 'Every new concept accompanied by etudes and songs for practice.'

systems.name: Pedagogical Organization (A-Lessons / B-Lessons)
systems.summary: Fisher's distinctive teaching structure: parallel A-lessons (chords/harmony) + B-lessons (improvisation) across his three volumes. Each chapter has a concept → etude → song-application. Documentation-only for v1 engine; may surface in a future practice-mode UI.

traversal_rules.name: A-lessons and B-lessons run in parallel
traversal_rules.summary: A1/B1, A2/B2, etc. are paired but independent tracks.

references.source: plans/fisher-system-rebuild.md (predecessor session 260521-aware-nebula)
references.citation: Fisher Vol 1-3 (Alfred Publishing) structure.

systems.name: Etude- and Song-Driven Practice
systems.summary: Fisher's commitment to demonstrating every concept with both an etude (mechanical) AND a full song (musical context). Composed tunes ('Noah's Groove', 'Ruby My Deerfly', 'Modelicious', 'Blue Tofu') are pedagogical artifacts as much as music.

traversal_rules.name: Etudes practiced before songs
traversal_rules.summary: Mechanical drilling precedes musical application.

masters.name: Jens Larsen
masters.biography: Jens Larsen is a Danish jazz guitarist and educator active in the modern jazz pedagogical tradition. Through book-length works in the Fundamental Changes series and an extensive online presence, his teaching codifies a structured vocabulary of arpeggio substitutions, chromatic enclosures, and altered/diminished scale applications, organized around shared-tone analysis and voice-leading discipline. Biography placeholder; refine with maintainer review on next pass.

principles.name: Substitute arpeggios by shared-tone preservation, not by root
principles.summary: The improviser does not arpeggiate the root chord; instead they select the substitute arpeggio whose notes maximally overlap the underlying chord's guide tones (3rd and 7th) and color tones (9, 11, 13, altered extensions). The canonical example is G#m7b5 over Bb7alt: G# preserves F (the b7 of Bb), and the m7b5 arpeggio efficiently expresses the altered dominant's b9, #9, b13 sonority. The hierarchy is 3rd-rooted > 5th-rooted > 7th-rooted; each step trades shared-tones for color.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Chapter 1 (Diatonic Substitution) establishes the framework; Chapter 4 (Altered Scale) develops the m7b5-on-3rd-of-altered-dominant case explicitly with the Bb7alt → G#m7b5 example on p.54.

principles.name: Chromatic enclosure as the default approach to chord tones
principles.summary: Target chord tones are approached not directly but via chromatic neighbors above and below, encircling the target before resolving onto it. This produces the 'bebop-line' surface texture but Larsen frames it pedagogically: the enclosure construction is taught as a shape (e.g. upper-chromatic + lower-chromatic + target) that can be transposed across chord tones, scale degrees, and harmonic functions. The shape generalizes; the specific notes follow from the underlying harmony.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Chapter 2 (Chromatic Enclosures) is the dedicated treatment; enclosures recur in Chapters 3-6 as a connective device across all subsequent material.

principles.name: Diminished symmetry generates upper-structure color over altered dominants
principles.summary: Four major triads embedded within each half-whole diminished scale (a minor-third-stack symmetry) become upper-structure devices for altered dominant tension. The line player selects among these triads to color the underlying altered dominant; the symmetry guarantees that each triad preserves the dominant's b9/#9/#11/13 sonority differently. Taught as a color palette, not a mechanical scale fragment.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Chapter 5 (Half-Whole Diminished Scale) develops the four-triad framework; Chapter 6's solo analysis demonstrates application in cadential contexts.

principles.name: Voice-leading via common tone, contrary motion, or encirclement
principles.summary: Every melodic move between phrases or chord changes must be justified by one of three voice-leading principles: a shared common tone held across the change, contrary motion (line moves opposite to the bass or to an implied second voice), or encirclement (line approaches the next target tone by chromatic neighbors). Large intervals must be balanced by stepwise resolution in the opposite direction. This is the criterion by which the book asks students to evaluate every melodic choice.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes, 2018.
references.citation: Counterpoint rule (large interval → stepwise opposite-direction resolution) appears across multiple chapters as a consistent connective tissue; Chapter 6 makes the voice-leading-as-criterion claim explicit through bar-by-bar solo analysis.

works.title: Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts
works.summary: Pedagogical text on improvisation as a unified harmonic and melodic system. Six chapters proceed cumulatively from diatonic substitution (Ch. 1), through chromatic enclosures (Ch. 2), harmonic minor on the V chord (Ch. 3), the altered scale (Ch. 4), and the half-whole diminished scale (Ch. 5), to a complete solo analysis integrating all prior material (Ch. 6). The book's central premise is that every arpeggio and scale choice must be grounded in shared tones with the underlying chord; voice-leading by common tone, contrary motion, or encirclement is the criterion by which the book asks students to evaluate every melodic choice.

references.source: Larsen, Jens. Modern Jazz Guitar Concepts. Fundamental Changes Music Publishing, 2018.
references.citation: Primary source for the four derived systems. Chapter-loop run 2026-05-28T15-05-20-larsen-modern-jazz-guitar-concepts; derived/STATEMENT.md and derived/systems-draft.json under plugin/data/masters-corpus/jens-larsen/modern-jazz-guitar-concepts/.

systems.name: Harmonic Substitution Lattice
systems.summary: The book's central system: a graph of diatonic and non-diatonic arpeggios organized by their shared-tone relationships to underlying chord tones. Members are the substitution arpeggio types available over each chord quality. Traversal rules govern moves between arpeggios within a bar or across changes — primarily by common tone, guide-tone preservation, or root-interval relationship (3rd, 5th, 7th). Modification rules govern how a given arpeggio is altered: shell-voicing reduction, reframing pentatonics as extended arpeggios, scale rerooting for non-diatonic options, chord-tone omission, and avoidance of unintended sus4 colors. Established in Chapter 1, this system is the lens through which the entire book operates.

traversal_rules.name: Guide-Tone Arpeggio Selection
traversal_rules.summary: Select the substitute arpeggio whose notes contain the chord's defining 3rd and 7th intervals.

systems.name: Chromatic Enclosure System
systems.summary: Governs how non-chord chromatic and diatonic passing tones are organized around target chord tones to produce characteristic bebop and hardbop melodic language. Members are enclosure construction types and resolution placement positions. Traversal rules specify where chromatic notes land in the rhythmic grid (off-beats) and how the improviser moves from one enclosure to the next target note. Modification rules govern when a diatonic substitute replaces a chromatic note and how beat-2 placement displaces resolution to beat 4 for a modern unresolved effect.

members.name: Diatonic Note Substitution for Missing Half-Step

traversal_rules.name: Chromatic Notes on Off-Beats
traversal_rules.summary: Place all chromatic passing notes on off-beats, keeping strong chord tones on the beat.

traversal_rules.name: Enclosure Must Target Chord / Scale Tone
traversal_rules.summary: Every enclosure must resolve to a chord or scale tone; enclosures floating free of harmonic targets are forbidden.

traversal_rules.name: Beat-2 Enclosure Displacement
traversal_rules.summary: Place an enclosure on beat 2 so it resolves on beat 4, producing a Metheny-esque unresolved sound.

traversal_rules.summary: Use hammer-ons, pull-offs, and slides so chromatic passing notes remain soft while scale tones are accented.

systems.name: Diminished Symmetry Triad System
systems.summary: Exploits the half-whole diminished scale's minor-third symmetry to generate the four embedded major triads and deploy them as upper-structure melodic material over dominant 7th chords (notably the 13b9 voicing). Members are the four major triads and their inversions. Traversal rules govern common-tone voice-leading, encirclement, and positional cycling. Modification rules apply the minor-third symmetry axiom to transpose any shape by m3, select triad pairs for specific extensions, and bridge to the altered scale via the B major triad.

traversal_rules.name: Minor-Third Symmetry Rotation
traversal_rules.summary: Transpose any triad shape or arpeggio by minor thirds to access all four positions within the diminished scale.

traversal_rules.name: Positional Cycling Before Neck Cross

systems.name: Melodic Line Construction System
systems.summary: A system for assembling complete jazz improvisation lines from named melodic gestures: ascending arpeggio + descending scale (the master's signature shape), Coltrane 1-2-3-5 and 1-b3-4-5 groupings, motif repetition with alteration, and the counterpoint rule that large intervals resolve stepwise in the opposite direction. Traversal rules specify gesture-to-gesture connections; modification rules govern rhythmic subdivision variation, scale-tone interpolation, and motivic alteration. This system wraps the harmonic substitution lattice and chromatic enclosure systems in a procedural envelope.

traversal_rules.name: Target-Note Approach
traversal_rules.summary: Organize the line by working backward from a specific chord tone to reach on the next strong beat.

traversal_rules.name: Motif Sequencing Across Chord Changes

traversal_rules.name: Counterpoint Large-Interval Stepwise Resolution
traversal_rules.summary: After a large ascending interval, resolve the line stepwise in the opposite (descending) direction.

masters.name: George Benson
masters.biography: Jazz guitarist and vocalist whose recording career spans from the Jack McDuff trio (early 1960s) through the soul-jazz crossover era of Breezin' (1976) to the vocal-pop work that defined his commercial peak. Originally trained as a bebop single-line player, Benson's harmonic vocabulary draws on tertian chord construction, the modal-substitution catalog of the natural-major harmonic field, and the chromatic color scales (harmonic minor, melodic minor, harmonic major) layered over diatonic progressions. The seven-volume George Benson Method (Jupiter Musical Group, 2019, co-authored with Peter Farrell) is the canonical written distillation of his playing approach. Vol. 1 (Chord Construction & Beginning Harmony) is the foundation; Vols. 2-7 build on it through advanced harmony, technique, approach tones, melodic-minor world, the Giant Lines, and the blues.

works.title: The George Benson Method, Vol. 1 — Chord Construction & Beginning Harmony
works.summary: Vol. 1 is a strict bottom-up curriculum that moves from notation literacy to a working theory of diatonic harmony on the guitar. Chapters 1-3 establish notational vocabulary, the diatonic/chromatic interval system, and the Circle of Fourths/Fifths as the system of key relationships. Chapter 4 — the densest chapter — constructs the chord universe by tertian stacking (Triads, Tetrads, Extended Chords). Chapters 5-6 organize those chords into harmonic fields and the seven modes and prescribe how to practice them. Chapter 7 extends the field idea to Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, and Harmonic Major as coloristic devices layered over existing chords rather than as progression engines. The book's load-bearing thesis: the fretboard is a matrix to be visualized chord-first, organized into four root-string zones (6th/5th/4th/3rd-strings, the last only for triads), anchored by bass-note Guide Notes.

references.source: Benson, George and Peter Farrell. The George Benson Method, Vol. 1 — Chord Construction & Beginning Harmony. Jupiter Musical Group Ltd., 2019.
references.citation: 319 PDF pages; book pages 1-292. Vol. 1 of a 7-volume series.

systems.name: Fretboard-as-Matrix Visualization System
systems.summary: Benson's load-bearing organizing system: the fretboard is a matrix to be navigated chord-first, with arpeggios, scales, and phrases derived from chord knowledge rather than the reverse. The Root splits the fretboard into left and right sides, the layout repeats at the 12th fret, and two Guide Notes on the 5th and 6th strings serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape. Chord shapes are organized into four root-string zones (6th, 5th, 4th, and 3rd-string for triads only). A foundational methodological commitment runs through Chapters 6 and 7: locate the bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings first, and defer 4th-string voicings until those are mastered. The book also prescribes a Study Devices practice protocol (one key at a time, always with a rhythmic groove, mastery before key changes, 6th/5th-string voicings before 4th-string ones) as a practice prescription that surrounds the system rather than functioning as a voicing-engine rule.

members.name: 6th-String Root Zone
members.summary: Chord shapes anchored to a root on the low-E (6th) string.

members.name: 5th-String Root Zone
members.summary: Chord shapes anchored to a root on the A (5th) string.

members.name: 4th-String Root Zone
members.summary: Chord shapes anchored to a root on the D (4th) string; deferred until 6th/5th-string fluency.

members.name: 3rd-String Root Zone (triads only)
members.summary: Triad-only zone anchored on the G string.

members.name: Guide Notes (5th and 6th strings)
members.summary: Memorized natural-note anchors that serve as the Root reference for every interval and chord shape.

members.name: 12th-Fret Octave Mirror
members.summary: The fretboard layout repeats at the 12th fret, allowing octave-equivalent transfer of any shape.

traversal_rules.name: Visualization via Bass Note
traversal_rules.summary: Anchor chord study to roots on the 5th and 6th strings rather than to scale shapes; move between zones by relocating the bass root.

locate the chord's bass-note root on the 5th and 6th strings only

traversal_rules.name: Defer 4th-String Voicings Until 6th/5th Mastery

traversal_rules.name: 12th-Fret Octave Mirror
traversal_rules.summary: Any shape can be relocated by the 12-fret octave repeat as a position shift.

traversal_rules.name: Proximity-Based Chord Selection
traversal_rules.summary: When moving between chords, prefer voicings close to one another on the fretboard.

modification_rules.name: Learn Shapes and Intervals Together
modification_rules.summary: Shapes and intervals must be learned as a single unit, anchored by the two Guide Notes that serve as the Root reference.

systems.name: Chord-Family System (Triads, Tetrads, Extended)
systems.summary: Chapter 4 partitions the chord universe into three families - Triads, Tetrads, and Extended Chords - all justified by tertian harmony (R, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13). The signature device is the Chord Skeleton: pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants. Voicings are continually shaped by a load-bearing instrument constraint: unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes.

members.name: Triads
members.summary: Four qualities (major, minor, diminished, augmented); three inversion positions (root, first, second).

members.name: Tetrads
members.summary: Seventh, Sixth, and Added-Tone subfamilies with a 4-position inversion framework.

members.name: Extended Chords
members.summary: 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths as tertian-with-exceptions.

members.name: Suspended Chords
members.summary: sus2/sus4 defined strictly as 3rd-replacement, visualized by anchoring to known dominant-7 shapes.

members.name: Chord Skeletons
members.summary: Pre-set Root/3rd/5th shapes on the 6th, 5th, and 4th strings with right-side and left-side variants.

traversal_rules.name: Smallest-Movement Voice Leading
traversal_rules.summary: Between adjacent chords, prefer the voicing that moves each voice the smallest distance.

references.quote_excerpt: voice leading is governed by the smallest-movement rule

traversal_rules.name: Sus Chords Anchored to Dominant-7 Shapes
traversal_rules.summary: Move into a suspended voicing by replacing the 3rd within a known dominant-7 shape.

traversal_rules.name: 4th-String Extended Voicings Derived From 6th/5th
traversal_rules.summary: Map 4th-string extended voicings as derivations from 6th- and 5th-string shapes, typically starting from the 7th or 6th of the chord.

modification_rules.name: Drop 2 / Drop 3 Restricted to Tetrads
modification_rules.summary: Drop 2 and Drop 3 voicing transformations are restricted to tetrads.

modification_rules.name: No Inversions for Symmetric Chords
modification_rules.summary: Diminished and augmented chords have no inversions because they are symmetric.

modification_rules.name: Omit the 5th in 13th Chords
modification_rules.summary: In 13th-chord voicings the 5th is almost always omitted.

modification_rules.name: No b9 Between Bass and Top on Major/Minor
modification_rules.summary: On major and minor chords, avoid a flat-9 interval between the bass note and the top note of the voicing.

references.quote_excerpt: avoiding a flat-9 between bass and top note on major/minor chords

modification_rules.name: #11 vs Natural 11 Rule
modification_rules.summary: Use #11 on major and dominant 7 chords to avoid clashing with the major 3rd; minor chords take the natural 11 freely.

references.quote_excerpt: use #11 on major and dominant 7 to avoid clashing with the major 3rd

modification_rules.name: Sus Is 3rd-Replacement Only
modification_rules.summary: Suspended chords are defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2 or sus4), never as additions.

references.quote_excerpt: defined strictly as 3rd-replacement (sus2/sus4)

modification_rules.name: Guitar Must Edit and Omit Notes
modification_rules.summary: Unlike the piano, the guitar must edit and omit notes; this constraint drives nearly every voicing choice.

modification_rules.name: Unmarked 7 Is Minor (Maj7 Must Be Marked)

systems.name: Harmonic Field System (Diatonic Modes and Substitutions)
systems.summary: Chapter 5 builds the harmonic field by stacking thirds on each degree of the natural major scale, yielding seven diatonic chords in Roman numerals (I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm) and pairing each degree with one of the seven modes (Ionian through Locrian). Scale notes are classified as Chord Notes, Extended Notes, Avoid Notes, and Characteristic Notes. Chapter 6 then prescribes a degree-by-degree substitution catalog grounded in modal character; listed extensions are melodic options, not mandatory voicing components.

traversal_rules.name: V Dominant-to-Sus Substitution
traversal_rules.summary: On the V degree, substitute G7 with Gsus9/Gsus13 (Dm/G as mixolydian) when a suspended-dominant color is desired.

references.quote_excerpt: G7 to Gsus9/Gsus13, Dm/G as mixolydian

traversal_rules.name: IIIm Phrygian Slash-Chord Recasting
traversal_rules.summary: On the IIIm degree, recast as slash voicings like Cmaj7/E, C/E, and Esus(b9) including the Jobim/Villa-Lobos Em7(b9).

references.quote_excerpt: Em7(b6), Cmaj7/E, C/E, Esus(b9) and the Jobim/Villa-Lobos Em7(b9)

traversal_rules.name: VIIm Locrian Recast as G7(9)/B
traversal_rules.summary: Recast the VIIm Bo as G7(9)/B as the locrian-compatible substitute.

references.quote_excerpt: recasting Bo as G7(9)/B

traversal_rules.name: I Degree C6/Cmaj7 Interchange
traversal_rules.summary: On the I degree, freely interchange C6 and Cmaj7, with a b9-conflict exception when the melody is the octave.

references.quote_excerpt: C6/Cmaj7 interchange with a b9-conflict exception

modification_rules.name: Modes Drive Both Harmony and Melody
modification_rules.summary: Each chord's correct scale choice and available tensions are determined by the mode paired with its degree.

references.quote_excerpt: modes drive both harmony and melody by revealing each chord's tensions

modification_rules.name: Avoid Notes Usable Mid-Phrase, Never as Endings
modification_rules.summary: Avoid Notes are usable mid-phrase but never as ending tones.

references.quote_excerpt: usable mid-phrase but never as ending tones

modification_rules.name: Listed Extensions Are Melodic Options, Not Voicing Requirements
modification_rules.summary: Extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord.

references.quote_excerpt: listed extended notes are melodic passing-tone options, not mandatory voicing components
extended notes need not sit in the voicing

modification_rules.name: Characteristic Notes Define Modal Identity
modification_rules.summary: Each mode is identified by characteristic notes that should be present (e.g., Lydian's #11, Mixolydian's b7).

references.quote_excerpt: those that give the mode its identity

modification_rules.name: 9th Forbidden on the VIIm Locrian Chord
modification_rules.summary: Forbid the 9th on the VII chord because it produces a b9 avoid note against the root.

references.quote_excerpt: forbidding the 9th on the VII chord because it produces a b9 avoid note

modification_rules.name: IIm Dorian Extensions With m6 Deferred
modification_rules.summary: On the IIm dorian degree, add Dm7(9)/(11)/(13) but defer m6.

modification_rules.name: VIm Aeolian Extensions and Slash Voicings
modification_rules.summary: On the VIm aeolian degree, allow Am7(9)/(11)/(b6) plus Dm7/A and Fmaj7/A slash voicings.

references.quote_excerpt: Am7(9)/(11)/(b6) plus Dm7/A and Fmaj7/A slash voicings

systems.name: Chromatic Color-Scale System (Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, Harmonic Major)
systems.summary: Chapter 7 builds its framework on the functional definition of the leading tone as the VII degree of the Natural Major Scale - the half-step tension the Natural Minor lacks. This absence motivated raising the natural minor's VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale, then the Melodic Minor Scale (treated per the Bach Scale as one symmetric form), and finally Rimsky-Korsakov's Harmonic Major Scale. These parent families each generate seven scale modes - deliberately called scale modes rather than harmonic fields - because their derived chord families are not progression-guiding structures but coloristic devices layered over existing chords.

members.name: Harmonic Minor Scale
members.summary: Im with min 3rd / min 6th / maj 7th; restores the leading tone to the natural minor.

members.name: Melodic Minor Scale (Bach Scale form)
members.summary: Im with min 3rd / maj 6th / maj 7th, treated as one symmetric form.

members.name: Harmonic Major Scale
members.summary: Rimsky-Korsakov's mid-20th-century scale: major scale with b6.

members.name: VIIm Locrian bb7 (from Harmonic Major)
members.summary: Sources o7(b13) and (11) colors.

traversal_rules.name: Color Scales Layer Over Existing Chords
traversal_rules.summary: Derived chord families are not progression-guiding; introduce them by layering coloristic tension over an existing diatonic chord, not by substituting the chord's function.

references.quote_excerpt: coloristic devices that add tension on top of existing chords

modification_rules.name: Leading-Tone Restoration Generates Color Scales
modification_rules.summary: Raising the natural minor's VII restores the leading tone and motivates the Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor, and Harmonic Major families as color sources.

references.quote_excerpt: raising its VII to produce the Harmonic Minor Scale

modification_rules.name: Each Derived Mode Specified by Avoid and Characteristic Notes
modification_rules.summary: Each derived scale mode is specified by its avoid notes and characteristic notes (e.g., Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th).

references.quote_excerpt: Am(maj7) avoids the minor 6th and features the major 7th

modification_rules.name: Extended Notes as Passing Tones, Not Voicing Requirements
modification_rules.summary: VERY IMPORTANT rule: extended notes need not sit in the voicing - many belong only as passing tones in the melody over the chord.

references.quote_excerpt: many belong only as passing tones in the melody

masters.name: Jimmy Bruno
masters.biography: Philadelphia-born jazz guitarist trained in the bebop tradition; sideman with Buddy Rich and Lionel Hampton in the 1970s before establishing his own quartet and a long pedagogical career. Bruno's published method centers on right-hand technique as a habit-formation problem: a three-rule directional picking system (down to higher string, up to lower, alternate on same string), justified by economy of motion, replaces strict alternate picking as the basis for jazz line playing. The Art of Picking (2002) is the canonical written distillation of this approach; companion volumes (notably Six Essential Fingerings for the Jazz Guitarist) supply the scale/fingering substrate the picking method is applied to.

works.title: The Art of Picking
works.summary: A compact picking-hand method built around three directional stroke rules — down when crossing to a higher string, up when crossing to a lower, alternate on the same string — justified by economy of motion. Chapters 1-2 establish the rules, the stroke-type vocabulary (down-up, up-down, in-between), and the no-anchor right-hand mechanics (elbow-driven motion, relaxed wrist, fingers off the strings/pick guard/body). Chapter 3 adds consecutive same-direction strokes as jazz vocabulary with a strict no-ringing constraint. Chapters 4-6 generalize the rule across textures: the six basic major-scale fingerings (V/H taxonomy: 6V2, 5V2, 6V4, 5V4, 6H2, 5H2), arpeggios (including Bruno's anti-sweep-picking polemic), and string skipping. Chapters 7-8 turn the mechanism toward musical material (bebop phrases, slurred-triplet articulation) and Chapter 9 codifies the sanctioned rule-breaks (big-band consecutive downs, rhythmic-break exceptions, rest-start stroke freedom). Chapter 10 closes with the meta-rule that picking is habit-formation, with repetition prescribed over analysis. The book's aesthetic axiom (Ch. 5): music drives picking, not the fretboard.

references.source: Bruno, Jimmy. The Art of Picking. Mel Bay Publications, 2002.
references.citation: 51-page method book; 10 chapters covering directional picking and its application to scales, arpeggios, string skipping, bebop phrasing, and articulations.

systems.name: Directional Picking
systems.summary: Bruno's central system: three directional stroke rules (down to higher string, up to lower string, alternate on same string) applied across all textures (scales, arpeggios, skips, bebop). Justified by economy of motion. The system enumerates the stroke-type vocabulary as members and codifies both the core rules and the sanctioned exceptions (rest start, triplet drill, big-band emphasis, rhythmic break, hard skip+accent override).

members.name: Down-up stroke
members.summary: Default stroke; trains downbeat accent.

members.name: Up-down stroke
members.summary: Inverted-accent stroke; upstroke carries the downbeat.

members.name: Mixed strokes
members.summary: Alternates accents between down and up.

members.name: In-between stroke
members.summary: Hardest stroke; pick moves between two strings (adjacent or skipped).

members.name: Consecutive down-stroke
members.summary: Multiple notes on adjacent strings picked with successive down-strokes.

members.name: Consecutive up-stroke
members.summary: Multiple notes on adjacent strings picked with successive up-strokes.

members.name: Same-string sixteenth reversal
members.summary: Direction reversal occurring on a single string with repeated notes.

traversal_rules.name: Down-stroke when crossing to a higher string
traversal_rules.summary: When the next note is on a higher string, use a down-stroke.

traversal_rules.name: Up-stroke when crossing to a lower string
traversal_rules.summary: When the next note is on a lower string, use an up-stroke.

traversal_rules.name: Alternate strokes on the same string
traversal_rules.summary: When two consecutive notes lie on the same string, alternate down and up.

masters.name: Peter Bernstein
masters.biography: Brooklyn-born jazz guitarist whose playing and teaching extend the post-bop lineage of Wes Montgomery, Grant Green, and Jim Hall. Sideman recordings with Lou Donaldson, Sonny Rollins, Diana Krall, and Larry Goldings situate his aesthetic in a melody-primary, narrative-driven approach to improvisation that treats jazz as a language rather than a vocabulary of scales. Bernstein teaches privately and at NYU; the 2023 master's thesis by Nico Moreno (also at NYU) is the canonical written distillation of his teaching, transcribed from Bernstein-consented lesson recordings. The masters.json entry is sourced from the Moreno thesis, not from a Bernstein-authored method.

works.title: An approach to Jazz Improvisation: A method by Peter Bernstein (as documented by Nico Moreno, NYU 2023)
works.summary: A master's-thesis-shaped distillation of Bernstein's private and NYU lessons, transcribed from Bernstein-consented lesson recordings. The document organizes Bernstein's teaching into eight conceptual themes: jazz as a language and the importance of narrative; how to learn a tune; the primacy of the melody; the relation between chords and melody; language, rhythm, and meaning; comping and supporting the melody; harmonic movement, tension, and substitutions; and expanding the vocabulary (creating ideas around target notes, creating ideas from a shape, the narrative of the solo). The thesis is explicit that Bernstein's own teaching lacks a strict methodical structure and that every concept is contextualized in specific musical material rather than presented as standalone theory — the document supplies the structure that Bernstein's informal pedagogy intentionally does not.

references.source: Moreno, Nico. "An approach to Jazz Improvisation: A method by Peter Bernstein." Master's Thesis, NYU Jazz Studies Program, 2023. Supervisor: Dr. David Schroeder.
references.citation: 30-page master's thesis transcribed from Bernstein-consented lesson recordings; covers eight conceptual themes plus closing remarks on Bernstein's aesthetic path.

systems.name: Melodic Narrative System (Jazz as Language)
systems.summary: Bernstein's load-bearing conceptual system: improvisation is language use, not scale deployment. Every concept is anchored to a specific tune rather than to an abstract harmonic exercise; the standard is a poem and the melody is its story. The system's central aesthetic - connected narrative outranks correctness-of-notes - unifies vocabulary acquisition (by listening and imitation), the practice unit (small phrases, not transcribed choruses), and the evaluative criterion for any played line (does it tell a story?). Contrast, silence, and rhymes are the narrative grammar; target notes are the structural landing points around which lines are built.

members.name: Tune-as-Poem
members.summary: Each standard treated as a poem whose syntax, word-assembly, and rhymes the player is studying.

members.name: Melody-as-Story
members.summary: Within the poem, the melody is the story itself; harmony and ornamentation are supporting customs.

members.name: Phrase as Unit of Vocabulary
members.summary: Small phrases (not full transcribed choruses) are the practice and recombination unit.

members.name: Target Note
members.summary: The melody's own landing points on chord tones - the structural basis for improvised lines.

members.name: Contrast
members.summary: Soft against loud, short against long, serious against lighthearted - the engine of narrative engagement.

members.name: Silence
members.summary: Spaces between notes serve as punctuation between ideas.

members.name: Rhyme
members.summary: Motifs connected across time via call-and-response or parallel tension-resolution.

traversal_rules.name: Build Lines That Lead to Melody's Target Notes
traversal_rules.summary: Identify the melody's target notes and build improvised lines that lead to them, starting simply and growing in complexity.

traversal_rules.name: Connected Narrative Outranks Correctness of Notes
traversal_rules.summary: Prefer ideas that relate to each other with purpose over notes that merely work over the chords.

traversal_rules.name: Contrast as Narrative Engine
traversal_rules.summary: Use contrast - soft against loud, short against long, serious against lighthearted - to sustain narrative engagement across a solo.

modification_rules.summary: End cadenzas with definitive, unambiguous endings under the performer's control (parking-a-car, ending-a-conversation analogies).

systems.name: Melody-Primary Improvisation System
systems.summary: Codifies the method's load-bearing pedagogical rule: melody is primary and harmony exists to serve it. The order-of-operations is explicit - the first practice task on any tune is to play the melody as a monophonic instrument, with attention to lyric syllables as a guide to articulation, before any harmonic embellishment. Target notes are the operational link between the melody and the harmony: when the melody lands on a specific chord tone, the player notes it and lets it dictate voicing choice. The 'juggling on a unicycle' maxim warns against texture-overload at the cost of melodic feel.

members.name: Monophonic Melody Delivery
members.summary: Play the tune as a single line with attention to lyric syllables.

members.name: Conservative Shell Voicing
members.summary: Root-3-7 shell voicings as the chord layer added after melody mastery.

members.name: Extension and Signature Run Layer
members.summary: Extensions and signature runs added only after the melody-chord relationship is heard.

members.name: Melody Target Note
members.summary: The specific chord tone the melody lands on; dictates voicing choice.

systems.name: Comping Aesthetic System (Dressing the Notes)
systems.summary: Applies the melody-first hierarchy to accompaniment via the signature 'dressing the notes' metaphor: comping's job is to give each melody note the right outfit so it feels important. The concrete practice rule is to begin with root-3-7 voicings only, internalize their relationship to the melody, and add extensions only after that relationship is heard. The chapter prescribes a two-phase comping-construction procedure - design a guide-tone line per chord as a secondary melody, then fill the inner voices to dress those guide tones. Tension and resolution are generated even inside a single sustained chord, and rhythm in comping is elevated to equal status with voicing choice.

systems.name: Vocabulary Development System (Recursive Variation and Target-Note Practice)
systems.summary: The apparatus by which a player expands the internalized library of phrases that improvisation recombines. Vocabulary is acquired the way children learn to speak - by listening and imitation - and creativity is defined operationally as recursive variation: ten ideas from one, then ten from each of those. The chapter enumerates three named variation methods (ear-led free variation, phrases built around a single target note, phrases around two simultaneous target notes) and pairs them with a permissive chromatic stance under the 'party for the target' analogy: diatonic notes are family, chromatic notes are friends and neighbors. The system closes with two practice meta-rules - practice away from the instrument so imagination leads the fingers, and learn classic lines first then progressively abstract them.

masters.name: Mickey Baker
masters.biography: Kentucky-born guitarist whose recording career bridged the 1950s R&B/early-rock-and-roll session world (Mickey & Sylvia's "Love Is Strange", Atlantic and Savoy sessions for Ray Charles, Big Joe Turner, Ruth Brown, LaVern Baker) and the Paris-based jazz expat community where he lived from the 1960s onward. The Complete Course in Jazz Guitar (Lewis Music Publishing Co., 1955) — universally known as Mickey Baker's Vol. 1 — is among the most-cited and most-used jazz-guitar method books ever written; its 26 lessons introduce the working-musician's chord vocabulary (the "Baker chords"), substitution lattice, comping and rhythm patterns, and single-note line vocabulary as a unified practical system rather than a theoretical exposition. A Volume 2 followed.

works.title: Mickey Baker's Complete Course in Jazz Guitar, Volume 1
works.summary: 26-lesson method book that introduces the jazz-guitar working-musician vocabulary as a practical system. The book is famous for its by-ear-first, voicing-first approach: Lessons 1-7 establish the core chord vocabulary (the "Baker chords" — major 6/major 7, minor 6/minor 7, dominant 7 + extensions 9, 13, +5, -5, sus); Lessons 8-12 build the harmonic-substitution lattice (relative-name pairings like Gma6 = Emi7, tritone and diminished subs, walking-bass lines); Lessons 13-18 develop comping rhythm patterns and vamp textures; Lessons 19-26 cover single-note line vocabulary, riff construction, and the deliberate use of lines that are 'foreign' to the underlying changes. The book's aesthetic axiom is practical-over-theoretical: sound-driven content selection, working-musician-tested voicings, and explicit rejection of formula-derived harmonic abstraction. A Volume 2 followed and could be added as a sibling work in a later run.

references.source: Baker, Mickey. Complete Course in Jazz Guitar, Volume 1. Lewis Music Publishing Co. (Amsco / Music Sales), 1955.
references.citation: 65-page method book; 26 numbered lessons. OCR-sourced via the cyberpower OCR branch (#315/#316/#318); transcripts retain tesseract+qwen2.5vl artifacts that survive into chapter quotes (see chapter-file frontmatter).

systems.name: Numbered Chord Vocabulary System (the 26-to-30 Catalog)
systems.summary: Baker's load-bearing taxonomic system: the guitar's chord universe is pruned to 26 essential shapes (Chapter 1), later extended to 30 by the addition of open-string-root horn-backing voicings (Chapter 5, Chords 27-30). Chord shapes are referenced by number (No. 1 through No. 30) rather than by Roman-numeral function, drilled chromatically up and down the neck so the same shape is internalized in every key, and cross-referenced via circled-number annotations over every progression diagram from Lesson 1 onward. The first six chords (No. 1-6) are restricted to the four foundational keys (F, G, Ab, Bb) and form the rhythm palette of Part One; the 9-12 group opens the second three-key cluster (C, Db, Eb) and installs the substitution menu (ma7/6/9, mi6/7/9, 13, 9, 7#5+9, 13#5+9, 7b5, 11, 13b9); and Chords 27-30 are open-string-root vamp shapes flagged as key-specific exceptions to the otherwise-universal transposition mandate. The system's operating principle is transposition-as-mastery: every voicing must be physically rewritten into the student's manuscript notebook in every key, with practical target lists (seven keys for vamps, six for intros, five horn-friendly keys for blues runs) contracting the universal mandate to working-guitarist reality.
